actors.name: EVM Caller (Smart Contract / EOA)
actors.description: Any smart contract or externally-owned account that invokes the p256verify precompile at address 0x100 via CALL/STATICCALL. This is an untrusted actor that supplies arbitrary 160-byte input to the precompile.

actors.name: p256verify Precompile
actors.description: The EIP-7951 precompile deployed at address 0x100, responsible for secp256r1 ECDSA signature verification. It is part of the trusted EVM execution environment.

actors.name: EVM Runtime / Execution Client
actors.description: The Ethereum execution client (e.g. Geth, Nethermind, Besu, Reth) that implements the precompile. It is trusted to correctly implement the secp256r1 cryptographic operations and gas metering.

actors.name: Block Proposer
actors.description: A validator selected to propose a beacon block for a given slot. The proposer constructs the block including execution payload and blob KZG commitments. Proposers are economically incentivized via staking but are not unconditionally trusted.

actors.name: Beacon Chain Validator
actors.description: A participant in the beacon chain consensus that attests to blocks and participates in justification/finalization. Validators collectively enforce protocol rules through attestation and slashing conditions.

actors.name: Execution Engine
actors.description: The execution-layer engine (Engine API endpoint) that verifies execution payloads. The beacon chain trusts this engine to validate payload consistency, including versioned hashes derived from blob KZG commitments.

actors.name: Network Peer
actors.description: Any node participating in the peer-to-peer gossip network. Peers use fork digests for domain separation and topic subscription. They are untrusted and may attempt to inject messages from incompatible forks.

actors.name: Blob Schedule Configuration
actors.description: The protocol-level configuration (BLOB_SCHEDULE) that defines epoch-based blob parameter transitions. This is a governance/protocol actor whose entries are trusted as canonical once embedded in the consensus spec.

trust_boundaries.description: Untrusted EVM caller provides arbitrary calldata to the p256verify precompile. The precompile must safely handle any 160-byte (or non-160-byte) input without reverting. This is the primary attack surface for EIP-7951.

The precompile returns a result (32-byte success or empty bytes for failure) to the caller. The caller must correctly interpret the return value; a malicious or buggy caller may misinterpret empty output as success.
The execution client provides gas context and calldata to the precompile. The precompile trusts the runtime for correct gas accounting and memory management.

The proposer submits a beacon block (including execution payload and blob commitments) to validators. Validators must independently verify all assertions (parent hash chain, randao, timestamp, blob count limits) and reject invalid blocks.

The proposer's execution payload is forwarded to the execution engine via verify_and_notify_new_payload. The execution engine is trusted to correctly validate payload consistency and state transitions.

The execution engine returns a validity verdict to the consensus layer. Validators trust this verdict to determine whether the execution payload is valid. A compromised execution engine could cause validators to accept invalid state transitions.

Network peers exchange messages on gossip topics identified by fork digests. The fork digest (now incorporating blob parameters via EIP-7892) acts as a domain separator. Peers from incompatible configurations should be automatically segregated.

The BLOB_SCHEDULE configuration determines maximum blob counts per epoch. Validators must correctly look up the schedule to enforce blob limits. Misconfigured or divergent schedules between clients would cause consensus failures.

Validators rely on the proposer_lookahead state field (EIP-7917) to know future proposers. This pre-computation means all validators can predict upcoming proposers, creating a trade-off between protocol efficiency and proposer privacy.

assumptions.text: The secp256r1 curve parameters (field modulus p, subgroup order n, base point G, cofactor 1) as defined in NIST SP 800-186 are correct and cryptographically secure.

The p256verify precompile implementation performs mathematically correct modular arithmetic, scalar multiplication, and point addition over the secp256r1 group. Bugs in these operations could cause consensus splits between clients.

Constant-time execution is NOT guaranteed by EIP-7951. The spec explicitly states that implementations are not required to use constant-time algorithms, meaning timing side-channels may leak information about private keys to co-located observers.
The precompile MUST NOT revert under any circumstances (except out-of-gas). Invalid inputs return empty bytes rather than causing a revert, ensuring callers can always handle failure gracefully.

The fixed gas cost of 6900 is assumed to be sufficient to prevent the precompile from being used as a denial-of-service vector. The gas cost must cover worst-case computational complexity across all client implementations.

Input validation checks MUST be performed in a specific sequential order (length, r bounds, s bounds, qx bounds, qy bounds, curve equation, infinity check) to ensure deterministic behavior across all client implementations.
The point-at-infinity check on R' during signature verification is mandatory (fixes RIP-7212 vulnerability). Omitting this check leads to implementation-dependent non-deterministic behavior.

The signature comparison must use modular reduction (r' == r mod n) rather than direct equality (fixes RIP-7212 vulnerability). Direct comparison fails for edge-case x-coordinates exceeding the subgroup order.

The execution engine (Engine API) is assumed to be a trusted, correct component that faithfully validates execution payloads. A bug or compromise in the execution engine bypasses all consensus-layer protections.
BLOB_SCHEDULE entries are assumed to have unique, monotonically meaningful epoch values, all >= FULU_FORK_EPOCH. Duplicate or out-of-order entries could cause ambiguous blob parameter resolution.
All execution clients implement identical BLOB_SCHEDULE configurations. Divergent schedules between clients would cause consensus failures at the epoch boundaries where blob parameters change.

The proposer lookahead (EIP-7917) shifts correctly at epoch boundaries, maintaining exactly (MIN_SEED_LOOKAHEAD + 1) * SLOTS_PER_EPOCH entries. Off-by-one errors in the shift operation could assign wrong proposers.

The fork digest XOR with blob parameter hash (EIP-7892) provides sufficient domain separation to prevent cross-fork message confusion. The 4-byte truncation is assumed to have negligible collision probability for practical configurations.

The epoch processing pipeline (15 sequential sub-processes) must execute in strict order. State mutations from earlier steps are visible to later steps, and reordering would produce incorrect state transitions.
Proposer indices are deterministically derived from the seed, active validator set, and slot. All honest validators compute identical proposer assignments given the same state.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could submit a forged signature (h, r, s) with a valid public key (qx, qy) to the precompile, attempting to impersonate the key holder. If the precompile has a bug in validation or verification logic, it could accept an invalid signature as valid.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The precompile implements comprehensive input validation (SG-002) and standard ECDSA verification (SG-003) including the RIP-7212 vulnerability fixes (point-at-infinity check, modular comparison). Callers must still verify the returned value is 0x01.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious caller could craft inputs that exploit edge cases in the secp256r1 arithmetic: zero-value r or s, points not on the curve, the point at infinity, or field elements exceeding the modulus. These could trigger undefined behavior in buggy implementations.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Sequential input validation (SG-002) rejects out-of-range r/s values, out-of-range coordinates, off-curve points, and the point at infinity before verification begins. The ordered validation ensures deterministic rejection across all client implementations.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could invoke the precompile repeatedly with crafted inputs designed to maximize computational cost, attempting to slow block processing. Since gas is fixed at 6900 regardless of execution path, inputs that fail early validation consume the same gas as successful verifications.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The fixed 6900 gas cost (SG-006) prevents gas-based DoS by ensuring callers always pay the full cost. However, if the actual worst-case computation exceeds what 6900 gas represents, it could create an underpriced operation exploitable for resource exhaustion.

stride_analysis.description: Since EIP-7951 explicitly does NOT require constant-time implementation, a co-located attacker (e.g., on the same physical machine as a validator) could perform timing side-channel attacks to extract information about the private key from the precompile's execution time variations.
stride_analysis.mitigation: No protocol-level mitigation. The specification acknowledges this by not mandating constant-time operations. Mitigation is left to individual client implementations and deployment hardening. This is a known trade-off favoring performance over side-channel resistance.

stride_analysis.description: A smart contract caller could misinterpret the precompile's return value. The precompile returns empty bytes (0 bytes) on failure and 0x01 (32 bytes) on success. A buggy caller that checks only the return status (success/fail) of the CALL opcode rather than the returned data would incorrectly treat all non-reverting calls as successful signature verifications.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Callers must explicitly check the returned data length (32 bytes) and value (0x01). The precompile's no-revert invariant (INV-001 in SG-001) means CALL always succeeds; the signature validity is encoded solely in the return data. Wallet and contract developers must be educated about this pattern.

stride_analysis.description: If different execution clients implement the secp256r1 arithmetic differently (e.g., different bignum libraries, different point multiplication algorithms), they could produce divergent results for edge-case inputs, causing a consensus split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification defines precise validation ordering (SG-002), exact byte layouts (SG-005), and specific vulnerability fixes (SG-003 INV-002, INV-003). Cross-client test vectors should be developed and validated. The ordered validation sequence is critical for determinism.

stride_analysis.description: A bug in the precompile implementation could allow an attacker to forge secp256r1 signatures, potentially enabling unauthorized actions in smart contracts that rely on P256 for access control (e.g., WebAuthn/Passkey-based wallets, HSM-backed authentication).
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification includes explicit fixes for known RIP-7212 vulnerabilities: mandatory point-at-infinity check on R' (SG-003 INV-002) and modular comparison r' == r (mod n) instead of direct equality (SG-003 INV-003). Rigorous testing against NIST test vectors is essential.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious proposer could construct a block with an execution payload whose parent_hash does not chain correctly, whose timestamp is inconsistent, or whose blob commitment count exceeds the current epoch's limit from BLOB_SCHEDULE.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Validators independently verify all payload assertions (SG-002 in Fulu beacon chain): parent hash chaining (INV-001), randao consistency (INV-002), timestamp correctness (INV-003), and blob count limits via get_blob_parameters (INV-004). Invalid blocks are rejected by attestation.

stride_analysis.description: A proposer could include the maximum allowed blob commitments (up to 21 after the second BLOB_SCHEDULE increase) to maximize block processing time. As blob limits increase via EIP-7892 without requiring a hard fork, a sudden increase could strain unprepared validators.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The BLOB_SCHEDULE defines maximum blob counts per epoch, and validators enforce these limits. The schedule is embedded in the consensus spec so all clients know the limits in advance. However, the dynamic nature of blob-only parameter changes means client operators must update configurations at epoch boundaries.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious entity could attempt to propose blocks for slots they are not assigned to. With EIP-7917's proposer lookahead, all participants know future proposers, making slot-stealing detectable but also making legitimate proposers targetable.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Proposer selection is deterministic and verifiable by all validators via the proposer_lookahead state field (SG-005). Blocks from unauthorized proposers are rejected during attestation. The deterministic seed derivation (SG-004) ensures all honest validators agree on the assignment.

stride_analysis.description: If the execution engine is compromised or buggy, it could accept invalid execution payloads (e.g., invalid state root, incorrect gas calculations, tampered transaction results). The consensus layer delegates payload validation entirely to the execution engine.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The consensus layer verifies structural properties (parent hash, randao, timestamp, blob counts) before forwarding to the execution engine (SG-002 in Fulu beacon chain). However, semantic validation of the execution payload state transition is entirely trusted to the execution engine. Multi-client diversity is the primary mitigation.

stride_analysis.description: The execution engine returns a boolean validity verdict for payloads. If the engine's verdict is incorrect (either false positive or false negative), there is limited ability to audit or repudiate the decision after the fact, as the execution engine's internal state is not directly observable by the consensus layer.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Multi-client execution environments provide implicit redundancy. If one execution engine produces different results from others, the consensus mechanism will naturally fork, and the majority-correct chain will be finalized. Post-hoc analysis tools can replay execution payloads.

stride_analysis.description: A network peer on an incompatible fork (different blob parameters) could attempt to inject messages into gossip topics. Without proper fork digest domain separation, validators could process messages from incompatible protocol versions.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The fork digest now incorporates blob parameters via XOR with hash(epoch + max_blobs_per_block) (SG-003 in Fulu beacon chain). This ensures different blob configurations produce different fork digests, automatically segregating peers with incompatible parameters on the gossip network.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious peer could flood the gossip network with messages using valid fork digests but invalid content, forcing validators to expend resources validating and rejecting them.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Standard gossip protocol rate limiting and peer scoring mechanisms apply. The fork digest serves only as a first filter for topic subscription; full message validation still occurs. Persistent offenders are deprioritized via peer scoring.

stride_analysis.description: If a client implementation has an incorrect BLOB_SCHEDULE configuration (wrong epoch values, wrong max_blobs_per_block, or missing entries), it would compute different blob parameter limits than other clients, causing the node to reject valid blocks or accept invalid ones, effectively forking itself off the network.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The BLOB_SCHEDULE is defined in the consensus spec and must be identical across all client implementations. The fallback to ELECTRA parameters when no schedule entry matches (SG-001 INV-004) provides a safe default. However, the dynamic scheduling mechanism (EIP-7892) requires careful coordination for each parameter change.

stride_analysis.description: The proposer lookahead (EIP-7917) makes future block proposers known to all network participants up to MIN_SEED_LOOKAHEAD + 1 epochs in advance. This enables targeted attacks against known future proposers: DDoS attacks to prevent them from proposing, MEV-related front-running, or social engineering.
stride_analysis.mitigation: This is an explicit design trade-off in EIP-7917, favoring protocol simplicity and efficiency over proposer privacy. Proposers can use infrastructure protections (DDoS mitigation, private relay networks). The lookahead period is bounded to limit the attack window. Single-secret leader election (SSLE) remains a long-term research direction.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary who knows the next proposer from the lookahead could launch a targeted DDoS attack against that proposer's infrastructure, causing them to miss their slot and the network to skip a block.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Proposers can use resilient infrastructure, VPNs, and relay networks. Missed slots are handled gracefully by the protocol (the chain simply skips to the next slot). The economic cost of staking provides some deterrent against proposer attacks. The bounded lookahead period limits advance planning time for attackers.

stride_analysis.description: If the proposer_lookahead shift operation (SG-008) contains an off-by-one error or the epoch computation is incorrect, a validator could be incorrectly assigned proposer duties, gaining the privilege to propose blocks in slots not legitimately theirs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The proposer_lookahead update logic (SG-008) uses explicit offset calculations and is part of the epoch processing pipeline. All validators compute identical lookahead state deterministically. An implementation bug would manifest as a consensus split, detectable through multi-client testing.

actors.name: Transaction Submitter (User / EOA / Contract)
actors.description: An external user, externally-owned account, or smart contract that constructs and submits a transaction to the Ethereum network with a specified gasLimit field. This is an untrusted actor who controls the transaction parameters including gasLimit.

actors.name: Transaction Pool (Mempool)
actors.description: The local transaction pool maintained by each execution client node. It receives incoming transactions, applies validation rules (including the EIP-7825 gas cap check), and holds valid transactions pending inclusion in a block. Each node's txpool operates independently.

actors.name: Block Builder / Proposer
actors.description: The entity responsible for constructing a candidate block from pending transactions in the txpool. In post-PBS Ethereum, this may be a specialized block builder or the validator itself. The block builder selects transactions and assembles them into a block.

actors.name: Block Validator (Consensus Participant)
actors.description: A node that receives a proposed block and independently validates it according to protocol rules, including verifying that every transaction in the block has gasLimit <= 16777216 (EIP-7825). Validators collectively enforce consensus by rejecting invalid blocks.

actors.name: Execution Engine / EVM
actors.description: The Ethereum Virtual Machine execution environment within each client that processes transactions within validated blocks. It executes smart contract code, including precompiled contracts like ModExp (0x05). It is trusted to correctly compute gas costs and state transitions.

actors.name: ModExp Precompile Caller (Smart Contract)
actors.description: Any smart contract that invokes the ModExp precompiled contract at address 0x05 via CALL/STATICCALL, supplying base, exponent, and modulus parameters. This is an untrusted actor that controls all input parameters to the precompile. Common use cases include RSA signature verification, zero-knowledge proof systems, and other cryptographic operations.

actors.name: ModExp Precompiled Contract (0x05)
actors.description: The modular exponentiation precompiled contract at address 0x05. It computes base^exponent mod modulus with gas cost determined by the EIP-7883 formula: max(500, floor(multiplication_complexity * iteration_count)). It is part of the trusted EVM execution environment.

actors.name: Protocol Governance (EIP Process)
actors.description: The Ethereum governance process that defines protocol constants such as the gas cap value (2^24 = 16777216) and the ModExp gas pricing formula. These constants are embedded in client implementations and are trusted as canonical once activated via a hard fork.

trust_boundaries.description: A transaction submitter sends a transaction with an arbitrary gasLimit to a node's transaction pool via RPC or P2P gossip. The txpool must validate the gasLimit against the protocol cap (16777216) before admitting the transaction. This is the first line of defense against oversized transactions.

The transaction pool provides validated transactions to the block builder for inclusion in a candidate block. The block builder trusts that the txpool has already filtered out transactions exceeding the gas cap. However, a malicious or buggy txpool implementation could pass through invalid transactions.

The block builder submits a candidate block containing transactions to validators. Validators must independently re-verify that every transaction in the block has gasLimit <= 16777216, providing a second enforcement layer. This defense-in-depth approach means a bypass at the txpool layer does not compromise the system.

After a block passes validation (including the gas cap check), the execution engine processes the transactions within the block. The execution engine trusts that block-level validation has been correctly performed before it begins execution.

An untrusted smart contract invokes the ModExp precompile with arbitrary base_length, modulus_length, exponent_length, and the corresponding data. The precompile must correctly compute gas costs using the EIP-7883 formula regardless of input values. This is the primary attack surface for gas mispricing exploits.

The ModExp precompile reports its gas consumption to the execution engine. The gas cost is deterministically computed from inputs using the EIP-7883 formula. The execution engine trusts this gas computation to be correct and deducts it from the caller's available gas.

Protocol governance defines the gas cap constant (16777216), the ModExp pricing formula, and the minimum gas floor (500). All execution clients must implement these identical constants. Divergent implementations would cause consensus splits.

In scenarios where transactions bypass the txpool (e.g., private transaction channels, direct-to-builder submission, or compromised nodes injecting transactions), the block validator serves as the ultimate enforcement point for the gas cap. Block validation must be comprehensive and independent of txpool filtering.

assumptions.text: The gas cap constant GAS_CAP = 16777216 (2^24) is a fixed protocol constant that cannot be changed without a hard fork. All clients implement the identical value.

The gas cap is independent of the block gas limit. Even if the block gas limit exceeds 16777216 (which it typically does at 30-40M), no individual transaction may specify a gasLimit above the cap. Multiple transactions can collectively exceed the cap within a single block.

The gas cap is enforced at exactly two layers: (1) transaction pool admission and (2) block validation. These two enforcement points provide defense-in-depth. A failure at layer 1 is caught at layer 2.

A transaction rejected at the txpool layer never reaches block validation (INV-009). This assumes the txpool correctly implements the rejection and does not have a race condition or bypass path that allows rejected transactions to leak into block building.

Block validation iterates over ALL transactions in the block before acceptance (INV-005). There is no early-exit optimization that could skip checking some transactions. A single violating transaction invalidates the entire block (INV-006).

The gas cap value (16777216) is the same at both the txpool validation layer and the block validation layer (INV-008). If these values diverge due to a client bug, transactions could pass one check but fail the other, leading to blocks that are built but rejected.
The gas cap applies uniformly to all transaction types (legacy, EIP-2930, EIP-1559, EIP-4844 blob transactions, etc.) regardless of the block gas limit set by validators (INV-010).

The ModExp gas cost formula (EIP-7883) is deterministic: for identical inputs, all client implementations compute the identical gas cost. Non-determinism would cause consensus failures where some nodes accept a block and others reject it due to different gas accounting.
The multiplication_complexity function has a minimum output of 16 for all inputs (INV-001 of SG-001 in EIP-7883). This ensures a baseline computational cost is always charged even for small operands.

For inputs with max(base_length, modulus_length) <= 32, the multiplication_complexity is fixed at 16, avoiding unnecessary computation of the word-based formula for small inputs (INV-002 of SG-001 in EIP-7883).
The iteration_count is always >= 1 for all non-trivial inputs (INV-001 of SG-002 in EIP-7883). The max(iteration_count, 1) clamp for large exponents ensures no zero-cost execution paths exist.

The large exponent multiplier has been doubled from 8 (EIP-2565) to 16 (EIP-7883) (INV-003 of SG-002). This corrects the underpricing of large-exponent operations, which was the primary vulnerability allowing resource exhaustion attacks.

The minimum gas floor for ModExp is 500 (raised from 200 in EIP-2565). This ensures that even trivial inputs incur a meaningful gas cost, preventing cheap abuse of the precompile. The formula is max(500, floor(complexity * iterations)) with no division by 3 (INV-002 of SG-003).

The removal of the division by 3 from the gas formula (previously present in EIP-2565 as gas = max(200, floor(complexity * iterations / 3))) represents a roughly 3x increase in gas costs for most operations, which is assumed to be a net improvement in security even at the cost of higher fees for legitimate users.

The EIP-7883 gas cost increase does not introduce new functionality or attack surfaces; it only makes existing operations more expensive. The security argument is that overpricing is strictly safer than underpricing for consensus-critical operations.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious actor could impersonate a legitimate user and submit transactions with gasLimit values just below the cap (e.g., 16777216) to maximize resource consumption per transaction. While the transaction is technically valid, the intent is adversarial resource exhaustion.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The gas cap itself limits damage. Each transaction can consume at most 16777216 gas, which is approximately half a typical block. Additional rate limiting, fee-based prioritization (EIP-1559 base fee), and txpool eviction policies provide further defense.

stride_analysis.description: A transaction submitter could craft a transaction with a gasLimit exceeding 16777216 to attempt to consume excessive block resources. If the txpool validation has an integer overflow, off-by-one error, or type confusion vulnerability in the comparison against the constant, the invalid transaction could be admitted.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The check is a simple integer comparison (gasLimit > 16777216) performed at the txpool layer (SG-001). The constant 2^24 is easily representable in all numeric types. Block-level validation (SG-002) provides a second check. Both layers must be bypassed for an attack to succeed.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could flood the txpool with high volumes of transactions at or near the gasLimit cap. While each transaction is individually valid, the aggregate volume could overwhelm txpool storage, eviction logic, and network bandwidth for transaction gossip.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Standard txpool size limits, mempool eviction policies (lowest fee eviction), per-sender nonce sequencing, and EIP-1559 base fee adjustments limit the effectiveness of txpool flooding. The gas cap does not make this worse than the status quo; it actually bounds the maximum size of individual transactions.

stride_analysis.description: A compromised or buggy txpool implementation could allow transactions with gasLimit > 16777216 to pass through to the block builder. If the block builder does not independently verify the gas cap (relying solely on the txpool's filtering), it could include invalid transactions in a candidate block.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Defense-in-depth design (INV-007): the gas cap is enforced at both txpool and block validation layers. Even if the txpool is bypassed, block validators will reject any block containing a transaction exceeding the cap. Block builders that produce invalid blocks waste their computational resources and forfeit MEV opportunities.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious block builder could intentionally construct a block containing a transaction with gasLimit > 16777216, hoping that some validators have a buggy implementation that skips the per-transaction gas cap check during block validation.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Block validation must iterate over ALL transactions (INV-005) and reject the entire block if any single transaction violates the cap (INV-006). The check is straightforward (integer comparison per transaction), and multi-client diversity ensures that a bug in one client is caught by honest nodes running other clients.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious block builder could create blocks with the maximum number of transactions, each at exactly the gasLimit cap (16777216), to maximize block validation time. Validators must check every transaction in the block, and a block packed with cap-sized transactions maximizes both the iteration count and the per-transaction gas execution load.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The block gas limit (typically 30M) naturally limits the number of transactions that can fit at the cap. At a 16777216 gas cap, only 1-2 transactions at the cap can fit in a standard block. The block gas limit thus provides an implicit bound on the aggregate validation and execution cost.

stride_analysis.description: A block builder that produces an invalid block (containing transactions exceeding the gas cap) cannot repudiate this action, since the block contents are signed and publicly visible. However, in private transaction channels (e.g., Flashbots), the builder might attempt to blame the transaction submitter for the invalid gasLimit.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Block validation is deterministic and independently verifiable by all nodes. The block builder bears responsibility for the contents of their block regardless of the transaction source. Signed blocks provide non-repudiation.

stride_analysis.description: If the gas cap check passes at the block validation layer but is not correctly enforced by the execution engine (e.g., the EVM allows a transaction to consume more gas than its stated gasLimit through a re-entrancy or gas stipend bug), the transaction could effectively bypass the cap during execution.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The gas cap applies to the transaction's declared gasLimit field, not to actual gas consumed. The EVM already enforces that a transaction cannot consume more gas than its gasLimit. The EIP-7825 cap simply adds an upper bound on what that declared gasLimit can be.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious smart contract could craft ModExp inputs with carefully chosen base_length, modulus_length, and exponent_length values designed to exploit edge cases in the gas cost calculation. For example, setting base_length or modulus_length to very large values while keeping actual data small, or using exponent_length > 32 to trigger the large-exponent path with minimal actual computational cost, attempting to find inputs where the gas charged is much less than actual computation time.
stride_analysis.mitigation: EIP-7883 significantly increases gas costs across the board: the minimum floor is raised from 200 to 500, the division by 3 is removed (effective 3x increase), and the large exponent multiplier is doubled from 8 to 16. The multiplication_complexity formula ensures costs scale quadratically with operand size (2 * words^2). These changes close the gap between charged gas and actual computation time.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could invoke the ModExp precompile with inputs that maximize computation time relative to the gas charged. Before EIP-7883, the gas formula underpriced certain operations (particularly large exponent operations), allowing an attacker to consume disproportionate CPU time per unit of gas spent. This is the primary threat that EIP-7883 is designed to address, as the underpricing becomes more dangerous if the block gas limit is increased.
stride_analysis.mitigation: EIP-7883 directly addresses this by: (1) raising the minimum gas from 200 to 500, (2) removing the /3 divisor making all operations 3x more expensive, (3) doubling the large exponent multiplier from 8 to 16, and (4) establishing a minimum multiplication_complexity of 16. Historical analysis shows approximately 99.69% of ModExp calls will see 150-200% cost increases, with worst-case scenarios seeing up to 9105% increases.

stride_analysis.description: The ModExp precompile processes potentially sensitive cryptographic inputs (RSA keys, ZK proof components). Execution time variations based on input values could leak information about the operands to co-located observers through timing side-channels.
stride_analysis.mitigation: ModExp operates on public blockchain data, so input confidentiality is not a primary concern (all inputs are visible on-chain). However, clients should still aim for consistent execution times to avoid creating exploitable timing oracles. The gas pricing changes in EIP-7883 do not address timing channels.

stride_analysis.description: If different client implementations compute different gas costs for the same ModExp inputs (due to integer arithmetic differences, rounding behavior in floor(), or different interpretations of the ceiling function in words calculation), they would disagree on whether a transaction has sufficient gas, causing a consensus split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EIP-7883 formulas use only integer arithmetic: ceil(max_length / 8) for word count, integer multiplication for complexity, and floor() for final cost. All operations are deterministic over integers. Multi-client test vectors and fuzzing should be used to verify cross-client consistency. The specification explicitly defines all branch conditions and formulas.

stride_analysis.description: If the gas cost calculation has an integer overflow vulnerability (e.g., 2 * words^2 overflowing for very large max_length values, or complexity * iterations overflowing), the computed gas could wrap to a small value, allowing an attacker to execute expensive ModExp operations for minimal gas cost.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Client implementations must use sufficiently large integer types (256-bit or arbitrary precision) for gas calculations. The inputs base_length, modulus_length, and exponent_length are bounded by the transaction's calldata size, which is in turn bounded by the block gas limit and the EIP-7825 transaction gas cap. These implicit bounds limit the maximum values that can appear in the formula.

stride_analysis.description: If the EIP activation occurs inconsistently across the network (some nodes activate at a different block/fork than others), transactions and blocks could be valid under one pricing regime but invalid under another, causing a chain split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Both EIP-7825 and EIP-7883 are activated via a coordinated hard fork (Fusaka). All clients must activate the new rules at the same block height. The hard fork coordination process includes extensive testnet validation and client readiness verification before mainnet activation.

stride_analysis.description: After the hard fork activates EIP-7883, smart contracts that previously relied on specific ModExp gas costs may fail or behave differently. Contract deployers could claim they were not adequately warned about the pricing change.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EIP process includes public discussion, testnet deployment, and community review periods. The backward-incompatible nature of the change is explicitly documented. Historical analysis shows the impact on existing contracts (99.69% of calls see 150-200% increases). Contracts should not hard-code gas amounts for precompile calls.

stride_analysis.description: In private transaction submission channels (e.g., Flashbots Protect, MEV Boost relays), transactions may bypass the public txpool entirely and be sent directly to a block builder. A malicious relay or builder could inject transactions with gasLimit > 16777216 that never went through txpool validation.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Block-level validation (SG-002 of EIP-7825) serves as the ultimate enforcement point regardless of how transactions enter the block. Every validator independently checks every transaction in every block. The two-layer enforcement (INV-007) ensures that bypassing the txpool layer does not compromise the system.

stride_analysis.description: In MEV-Boost or PBS architectures, the block builder is a separate entity from the proposer. A malicious builder could construct a block with gas-cap-violating transactions and submit it through a relay, hoping the proposer blindly signs without full validation.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Proposers (validators) must validate the block before signing. The MEV-Boost protocol requires that the relay verifies block validity. Even if the relay is compromised, the proposer's own validation and the network's block validation by attesting validators will reject invalid blocks.

actors.name: Beacon Node (Local)
actors.description: The local consensus-layer client executing state transitions, validating gossip messages, serving RPC requests, and managing fork upgrades. It is the primary trusted computing base for all subgraphs in the Fulu fork and P2P interface specifications.

actors.name: Peer Node (Remote)
actors.description: Any remote beacon node connected via the P2P network. Peers gossip data column sidecars, beacon blocks, and respond to RPC requests (DataColumnSidecarsByRange, DataColumnSidecarsByRoot). Peers are untrusted by default and must have all their messages validated.

actors.name: Block Proposer (Validator)
actors.description: A validator that constructs and signs beacon blocks containing blob KZG commitments. The proposer's identity is verified against the expected proposer schedule derived from the beacon state shuffling. Proposers are semi-trusted: authenticated by BLS signature but may act maliciously (e.g., equivocation).

actors.name: Execution Layer Client (Engine API)
actors.description: The local execution-layer client providing blob data via the engine_getBlobsV2 API. Used during distributed blob publishing (SG-010) to retrieve blobs matching KZG commitments. Fully trusted by the consensus layer; blobs from the local EL skip cryptographic verification.

actors.name: Gossip Network (libp2p GossipSub)
actors.description: The underlying GossipSub protocol layer that disseminates data column sidecars and beacon blocks across subnets. Messages arriving via gossip are fully untrusted and must pass multi-stage validation before acceptance and forwarding.

actors.name: RPC Requester (Remote Peer)
actors.description: A remote peer issuing ReqResp protocol requests such as DataColumnSidecarsByRange or DataColumnSidecarsByRoot. Request parameters are untrusted and must be validated against servable ranges and maximum response size limits.

actors.name: KZG Cryptographic Library
actors.description: The trusted KZG polynomial commitment scheme implementation providing verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. This is a foundational cryptographic trust anchor for data availability sampling. Assumed to be correctly implemented and cryptographically sound.

actors.name: Fork Scheduler / Configuration Constants
actors.description: The set of hardcoded fork epoch constants (FULU_FORK_EPOCH, ELECTRA_FORK_EPOCH, DENEB_FORK_EPOCH, etc.) and fork version constants (FULU_FORK_VERSION, etc.) embedded in all client implementations. These are trusted configuration parameters that must be identical across all nodes to maintain consensus.

trust_boundaries.description: Data column sidecars received via gossip subnets cross into the local node's validation pipeline. This is the primary untrusted-to-trusted boundary in the Fulu P2P interface. SG-006 (data_column_sidecar_gossip_validation) implements a 13-step sequential validation pipeline with REJECT, IGNORE, and ACCEPT outcomes before any message is forwarded to peers.

Block proposer's signed block header is verified during gossip validation (SG-006, q_check_proposer_sig and q_check_expected_proposer). The proposer claims an identity (proposer_index) that must match the expected proposer for the slot derived from the beacon state shuffling. BLS signature verification is the authentication gate.

Remote peers request data column sidecars via DataColumnSidecarsByRange (SG-008) and DataColumnSidecarsByRoot (SG-009). The local node must bound responses by MAX_REQUEST_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS and validate that requests fall within the data_column_serve_range to prevent resource exhaustion and information leakage.

The beacon node calls engine_getBlobsV2 on the local execution layer during distributed blob publishing (SG-010). Critically, blobs retrieved from the local EL skip cryptographic verification (INV-001 of SG-010), creating a direct trust dependency on EL integrity. A compromised EL could inject invalid blob data.

The local node publishes data column sidecars to gossip subnets it is subscribed to (custody or sampling) during distributed blob publishing (SG-010). Published sidecars must be valid and the anti-equivocation cache must be updated regardless of whether publishing occurs (INV-003 of SG-010).

ENR eth2 field exchange during peer discovery (SG-012). Peers advertise their fork digest and next fork version/epoch via ENR records. Incorrect or malicious ENR data could cause peering with incompatible nodes or prevent connection to compatible ones. BPO forks add complexity by not changing fork_version.

Fork epoch constants control state transitions (fork SG-001 fork_trigger, fork SG-002 upgrade_to_fulu) and fork version computation (P2P SG-001 compute_fork_version). These are configuration-level trust anchors: misconfiguration or divergence between clients causes chain splits. Fork epoch constants must be strictly ordered.

KZG proof verification (P2P SG-003 verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs) and Merkle inclusion proof verification (P2P SG-004 verify_data_column_sidecar_inclusion_proof) are the cryptographic trust anchors for data column integrity. The node trusts verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch, hash_tree_root, and is_valid_merkle_branch to be correct.

Deprecated blob sidecar protocols (P2P SG-011 blob_sidecars_by_range_deprecated, P2P SG-013 blob_sidecars_by_root_deprecated) during the Fulu transition period. Peers may still request blob sidecars via the old API. The local node must handle these gracefully, serving only pre-Fulu data and not penalizing peers for transition-boundary requests.

Subnet routing boundary (P2P SG-005 compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar). Data column sidecars must be received on the correct subnet (column_index % DATA_COLUMN_SIDECAR_SUBNET_COUNT == subnet_id). Misrouted sidecars are rejected early in the gossip validation pipeline (q_check_subnet in SG-006).

assumptions.text: Fork epoch constants (FULU_FORK_EPOCH, ELECTRA_FORK_EPOCH, DENEB_FORK_EPOCH, CAPELLA_FORK_EPOCH, BELLATRIX_FORK_EPOCH, ALTAIR_FORK_EPOCH) are correctly configured and strictly ordered (INV-001 of P2P SG-001). All nodes in the network share identical constant values. Any divergence causes a chain split.

The KZG commitment scheme (verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch) is cryptographically sound and correctly implemented. This is the foundational guarantee of data availability in the PeerDAS / Fulu data column sampling scheme. A break in KZG security would undermine the entire data availability layer.

The Merkle tree implementation (hash_tree_root, is_valid_merkle_branch, get_generalized_index, get_subtree_index) is correct and collision-resistant. The KZG_COMMITMENTS_INCLUSION_PROOF_DEPTH is fixed at 4 (INV-001 of P2P SG-004). This links kzg_commitments to the BeaconBlockBody root.

The local execution layer client (engine_getBlobsV2) returns authentic, untampered blob data. Blobs retrieved from the local EL skip cryptographic verification (INV-001 of P2P SG-010), creating a critical trust dependency. If the EL is compromised, invalid data columns could be published to the network.

The proposer shuffling algorithm deterministically produces the same expected proposer for each slot across all honest nodes running the same beacon state. This underpins the proposer_index verification in gossip validation (SG-006, q_check_expected_proposer). If shuffling computation is deferred, sidecars are IGNORED rather than REJECTED (INV-005 of P2P SG-006).

The anti-equivocation cache correctly deduplicates sidecars by the tuple (slot, proposer_index, sidecar.index) as specified in INV-004 of P2P SG-006. This prevents accepting conflicting data column sidecars from equivocating proposers. The cache must be updated regardless of whether publishing occurs (INV-003 of P2P SG-010).

The gossip validation pipeline (P2P SG-006) is executed atomically for each sidecar. Exactly one path from q_init to q_final is taken per sidecar (INV-003). All REJECT checks must fail before a message is forwarded (INV-001). IGNORE conditions allow re-processing later via queuing (INV-002).

Wall-clock time is reasonably synchronized across nodes. Slot timing checks in gossip validation (future slot IGNORE at q_check_future_slot, finalized slot IGNORE at q_check_finalized) depend on current_slot accuracy. Clock skew can cause premature rejection or delayed acceptance. ENR epoch (INV-004 of P2P SG-012) is also determined by wall-clock time, not sync state.

The fork upgrade function (upgrade_to_fulu, fork SG-002) is pure and deterministic. All fields from the pre-state (electra.BeaconState) are preserved in the post-state except fork and proposer_lookahead (INV-001). No side effects on the pre-state occur (INV-006). fork.previous_version correctly chains to pre.fork.current_version (INV-002).

NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS and DATA_COLUMN_SIDECAR_SUBNET_COUNT (128) are network-wide constants agreed upon by all nodes. The subnet computation (column_index % DATA_COLUMN_SIDECAR_SUBNET_COUNT) produces results in [0, 128) (INV-002 of P2P SG-005). Disagreement on these values would fragment the gossip network.

MAX_REQUEST_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS provides an effective upper bound on RPC response sizes for both DataColumnSidecarsByRange (INV-002 of P2P SG-008) and DataColumnSidecarsByRoot (INV-001 of P2P SG-009). This is the primary defense against resource exhaustion via RPC requests.

The finalized checkpoint is a reliable and agreed-upon anchor. finalized_checkpoint.epoch used in gossip validation (q_check_finalized in SG-006) and in RPC range computation (data_column_serve_range in SG-008, SG-009) reflects supermajority agreement. The finalized checkpoint must also be an ancestor of any accepted block (q_check_finalized_ancestor in SG-006).

During the Fulu transition period, the deprecated BlobSidecarsByRange (P2P SG-011) and BlobSidecarsByRoot (P2P SG-013) endpoints serve only pre-Fulu epoch data within the range [max(current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS, FULU_FORK_EPOCH), FULU_FORK_EPOCH). Peers are not penalized for requesting blob sidecars from FULU_FORK_EPOCH (INV-003 of both SG-011 and SG-013). These endpoints are fully deprecated after FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS.

BPO (Blob Parameter Only) forks do not change the fork version, only the fork epoch. ENR encoding (P2P SG-012) must handle this distinction correctly: for BPO forks, next_fork_version remains current_fork_version while next_fork_epoch is set to the planned epoch (INV-002 of P2P SG-012). Incorrect handling would cause peering incompatibility.

get_blob_parameters(epoch) returns epoch-dependent blob limits (max_blobs_per_block). This function is trusted to return correct and consistent parameters for each epoch across all nodes (INV-001 of P2P SG-007). Epoch-dependent parameters enable future parameter changes without hard forks.

The fork trigger (fork SG-001) only fires at epoch boundaries (slot % SLOTS_PER_EPOCH == 0) per INV-001, fires exactly once at FULU_FORK_EPOCH per INV-002, and is irreversible per INV-003. Once triggered, the state is permanently upgraded. This prevents fork oscillation attacks.

The proposer_lookahead field introduced in Fulu (EIP-7917) is freshly initialized via initialize_proposer_lookahead(pre) during the upgrade (INV-005 of fork SG-002). This new field is the only net-new addition to the BeaconState; all other fields are faithfully copied from the pre-state.

DataColumnSidecarsByRange responses are ordered by (slot, column_index) (INV-003 of P2P SG-008) and all sidecars of a block must be included if any are included (INV-004). Sidecars must be from the current fork choice chain (INV-005). Fork choice changes terminate the response stream.

Sidecars served via RPC should only come from blocks passing gossip validation (INV-004 of P2P SG-009) and should not be served for blocks failing the beacon-chain state transition (INV-005). This prevents serving invalid data to requesting peers.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious peer could forge a data column sidecar with a fabricated signed_block_header claiming to be from a legitimate block proposer. The sidecar would appear to originate from a valid proposer for the slot.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Gossip validation (SG-006) verifies the proposer BLS signature against the expected proposer for the slot at q_check_proposer_sig. Invalid signatures result in REJECT. Subsequently, q_check_expected_proposer verifies that the proposer_index matches the expected proposer derived from the beacon state shuffling.

stride_analysis.description: A peer could tamper with the data column contents (cells), KZG commitments, or KZG proofs within a sidecar before forwarding it on the gossip network. This could inject invalid data into the data availability layer.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Three-layer integrity verification: (1) verify_data_column_sidecar (P2P SG-002) checks structural validity -- index in [0, NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS), non-empty commitments, count within max_blobs_per_block, and equal-length arrays; (2) verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs (P2P SG-003) verifies cryptographic binding of cells to commitments via verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch; (3) verify_data_column_sidecar_inclusion_proof (P2P SG-004) verifies Merkle inclusion of kzg_commitments in the block body_root at depth 4.

stride_analysis.description: A block proposer could publish conflicting data column sidecars (equivocation) for the same slot, providing different data columns to different subnets, and then deny having produced one of the conflicting versions.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The anti-equivocation cache tracks sidecars by the tuple (slot, proposer_index, sidecar.index). Only the first valid sidecar for each tuple is accepted; duplicates are IGNORED (INV-004 of SG-006). The signed block header provides cryptographic non-repudiation evidence binding the proposer to the content.

stride_analysis.description: Gossip validation timing and rejection/ignore behavior could leak information about the local node's state (e.g., which blocks have been finalized, which parent blocks have been seen, current slot estimate).
stride_analysis.mitigation: The protocol uses IGNORE (rather than REJECT) for conditions that depend on local state: parent not seen (q_ignore_no_parent), future slot (q_ignore_future), pre-finalized slot (q_ignore_old), and duplicate sidecar (q_ignore_duplicate). This prevents peers from definitively inferring local node state from rejection messages. However, timing side-channels from the validation pipeline ordering may still exist.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could flood the gossip network with invalid data column sidecars, forcing all receiving nodes to execute the expensive 13-step validation pipeline (including BLS signature verification and KZG proof verification) before rejecting them.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Validation checks are ordered from cheapest to most expensive in SG-006: structural checks first (verify_data_column_sidecar), then subnet validation (cheap modulo operation), then slot timing checks, then proposer signature verification, then parent and fork choice checks, and finally KZG/Merkle proofs last. Early rejection at cheap steps avoids expensive cryptographic operations for most invalid messages.

stride_analysis.description: A non-proposer node could attempt to inject a sidecar claiming to be from a legitimate proposer, effectively acting as a block proposer without being scheduled for the slot.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The gossip validation pipeline verifies proposer_index against the expected proposer for the slot at q_check_expected_proposer, the final check before acceptance. If the shuffling computation is not yet available, the sidecar is IGNORED (not accepted), preventing privilege escalation. The BLS signature check at q_check_proposer_sig provides the cryptographic authentication layer.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could craft a valid-looking block header with a forged proposer_index, attempting to impersonate a scheduled proposer by guessing or reusing a BLS signature.
stride_analysis.mitigation: BLS signature verification of the signed_block_header cryptographically binds the proposer_index to the proposer's private key. Forging a BLS signature is computationally infeasible. The signature is checked (q_check_proposer_sig) before the proposer schedule check, ensuring identity authentication precedes authorization.

stride_analysis.description: A legitimate proposer could maliciously construct a block with more blob KZG commitments than allowed by max_blobs_per_block for the current epoch, or with mismatched array lengths in the sidecar (column vs. commitments vs. proofs).
stride_analysis.mitigation: P2P SG-007 (beacon_block_kzg_validation) enforces len(blob_kzg_commitments) <= get_blob_parameters(epoch).max_blobs_per_block. P2P SG-002 (verify_data_column_sidecar) enforces that column, kzg_commitments, and kzg_proofs all have equal length (INV-004). Blocks with zero commitments are explicitly allowed (INV-002 of P2P SG-007).

stride_analysis.description: A malicious peer could issue DataColumnSidecarsByRange (SG-008) or DataColumnSidecarsByRoot (SG-009) requests with very large count values or many column identifiers, causing the responding node to perform excessive disk I/O, computation, and network transmission.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Both RPC endpoints enforce MAX_REQUEST_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS as an upper bound. DataColumnSidecarsByRoot (SG-009) computes requested_columns_count = sum(len(r.columns) for r in request) and rejects if it exceeds the maximum. DataColumnSidecarsByRange (SG-008) bounds by count * NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS. The serve range is also bounded to recent epochs.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious RPC requester could craft requests targeting blocks outside the servable range, attempting to force the node to access historical data that may not be available, trigger error paths, or scan its full block storage.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Both endpoints compute a data_column_serve_range = [max(current_epoch - MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS, FULU_FORK_EPOCH), current_epoch]. Requests outside this range receive ResourceUnavailable(3) responses (SG-008) or are skipped (SG-009). For DataColumnSidecarsByRoot, blocks must also pass gossip validation before sidecars are served (INV-004 of SG-009).

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could probe which blocks the local node has stored by requesting specific block roots via DataColumnSidecarsByRoot (SG-009), systematically mapping the node's chain view and data availability.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification allows skipping identifiers where the block fails gossip validation or the epoch is too old. However, response patterns (presence vs. absence of sidecars, response timing) do leak information about the node's block inventory. This is inherent to the ReqResp protocol design and partially mitigated by the serve range restriction.

stride_analysis.description: If the local execution layer is compromised or has a bug, it could return tampered or incorrect blob data via engine_getBlobsV2. Since blobs from the local EL skip verification (INV-001 of SG-010), these tampered blobs would be converted to data columns and published to the gossip network as if valid.
stride_analysis.mitigation: This is a critical and intentional trust assumption in the protocol design. The local EL is trusted by construction. Mitigations are operational: ensure the EL client is authentic, up-to-date, and runs in a secure environment. Network-level redundancy provides a secondary defense -- other nodes will independently verify data columns received via gossip and reject invalid ones.

stride_analysis.description: If engine_getBlobsV2 fails, times out, or returns incomplete blob data (not all blobs matching the block's kzg_commitments are retrieved), the distributed blob publishing process aborts, potentially reducing data availability for the block across the network.
stride_analysis.mitigation: SG-010 explicitly handles this case: if not all blobs are retrieved, the process aborts gracefully at q_no_blobs. Other nodes performing the same distributed publishing flow provide redundancy. The abort does not crash the node and does not affect other node operations. The anti-equivocation cache is not polluted by a failed attempt.

stride_analysis.description: After publishing data column sidecars, a node could deny having published them if the published data is later found to be invalid or conflicting with other sidecars on the network.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Published sidecars contain the original proposer's signed_block_header which provides cryptographic non-repudiation at the proposer level. At the publisher level, the anti-equivocation cache (INV-003 of SG-010) must be updated regardless of whether publishing occurs, ensuring local state consistency and preventing the node from later publishing a conflicting sidecar.

stride_analysis.description: A node could attempt to publish data column sidecars on subnets it is not subscribed to (neither custody nor sampling), effectively acting as an unauthorized publisher and potentially influencing data availability beyond its designated role.
stride_analysis.mitigation: SG-010 explicitly checks subscription status at q_check_subscribed. Publishing only occurs on subnets the node is subscribed to via custody or sampling duties. Non-subscribed subnets skip the publish step and proceed directly to cache update. The gossip layer also enforces that messages are only accepted from peers subscribed to the topic.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious node could advertise a false ENR eth2 field, claiming to be on a different fork version or fork epoch than it actually runs. This could cause honest nodes to attempt peering with incompatible nodes, wasting connection slots and potentially partitioning the network.
stride_analysis.mitigation: ENR records are signed by the node's identity key, preventing third-party modification. However, a node can legitimately misrepresent its own fork status in its ENR. Defense relies on detecting incompatible behavior post-connection through protocol-level fork digest validation in the libp2p handshake and status message exchange.

stride_analysis.description: During BPO fork transitions (SG-012), incorrect ENR eth2 encoding could cause nodes to fail peer discovery or disconnect from the network. Since BPO forks do not change fork_version but do change fork_epoch, nodes with incorrect BPO handling could end up with incompatible ENR digests.
stride_analysis.mitigation: SG-012 explicitly handles BPO forks as a distinct code path: next_fork_version remains current_fork_version while next_fork_epoch is set to the planned epoch (INV-002). This ensures ENR compatibility during BPO transitions. The three-way branching (no fork, regular fork, BPO fork) covers all cases.

stride_analysis.description: If fork epoch constants are tampered with (e.g., via supply chain attack on client software or configuration file manipulation), the node would compute the wrong fork version for any given epoch (P2P SG-001), fork at the wrong time (fork SG-001), or produce an incorrect fork upgrade (fork SG-002), causing a chain split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Fork constants are hardcoded in client releases and verified through the Ethereum consensus specification. The compute_fork_version function (P2P SG-001) enforces strict ordering: GENESIS < ALTAIR < BELLATRIX < CAPELLA < DENEB < ELECTRA < FULU (INV-001). Multiple independent client implementations provide cross-validation. Exactly one branch is taken for any valid epoch (INV-002), and a valid Version is always returned (INV-003).

stride_analysis.description: A misconfigured FULU_FORK_EPOCH could cause the fork trigger (fork SG-001) to never fire, fire at the wrong epoch boundary, or fire multiple times, permanently preventing or corrupting the network upgrade.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Fork SG-001 enforces three critical invariants: INV-001 ensures fork only triggers at epoch boundaries (slot % SLOTS_PER_EPOCH == 0); INV-002 ensures it fires exactly once at FULU_FORK_EPOCH; INV-003 ensures irreversibility -- once triggered, the state is permanently upgraded and the fork cannot be rolled back.

stride_analysis.description: If the KZG library has a correctness bug allowing invalid proofs to pass verification (verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch returns true for invalid cell/commitment/proof tuples), tampered data column contents would be accepted as valid, completely undermining the data availability sampling scheme.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The KZG library is a critical trust anchor with multiple layers of assurance: formal verification of KZG implementations, multiple independent implementations (c-kzg, go-kzg, etc.), extensive test vectors from the Ethereum consensus spec test suite, and the underlying cryptographic hardness assumption of the KZG polynomial commitment scheme over BLS12-381.

stride_analysis.description: verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch is computationally expensive (pairing operations). An attacker could craft sidecars that pass all cheap structural and signature checks but require maximum-cost KZG verification before being ultimately rejected due to invalid proofs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The gossip validation pipeline (SG-006) places KZG verification (q_check_kzg_proofs) at step 11 of 13, near the end of the validation chain, after all cheaper checks have passed. This minimizes unnecessary KZG computation. However, a well-crafted sidecar that passes structural validation, subnet check, slot timing, signature verification, parent validation, ancestry check, and Merkle inclusion proof will still trigger the expensive KZG verification.

stride_analysis.description: During the Fulu transition period, peers could flood the deprecated BlobSidecarsByRange (SG-011) and BlobSidecarsByRoot (SG-013) endpoints with requests, consuming resources on nodes still supporting the transition protocol while the data is being phased out.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Both SG-011 and SG-013 define a narrowing serve range that eventually reaches zero. After FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS, the endpoints are fully deprecated and respond with empty lists or rejections (INV-001). The transition period is time-bounded. Standard ReqResp rate limiting also applies.

stride_analysis.description: During the transition period, response behavior to deprecated blob sidecar requests could reveal whether a node has pre-Fulu historical data, potentially aiding targeted attacks against archival nodes or nodes with specific pruning configurations.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification mandates that peers should not be penalized for requesting blob sidecars from FULU_FORK_EPOCH (INV-003 of both SG-011 and SG-013). Response behavior is deterministic based on the serve_range computation, which depends only on public information (current_epoch, FULU_FORK_EPOCH). This limits but does not fully eliminate information leakage about the node's data retention policy.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could publish data column sidecars on incorrect subnets (where column_index % DATA_COLUMN_SIDECAR_SUBNET_COUNT != actual subnet_id), causing receiving nodes to waste resources processing and validating messages that will ultimately be rejected at the subnet check.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Subnet validation (compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar(sidecar.index) != subnet_id) is performed at q_check_subnet, step 2 of the gossip validation pipeline (SG-006), immediately after structural validation. This is a cheap modulo operation that catches misrouted messages before any expensive cryptographic verification. The GossipSub protocol itself also provides topic-level filtering.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker subscribed to a specific subnet could inject sidecars with a column_index that correctly maps to that subnet (passing the modulo check) but references a different block, invalid proposer, or fabricated content, attempting to pollute the subnet with plausible-looking but invalid data.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Even if the subnet routing check passes, the full remaining 11 steps of the gossip validation pipeline must complete: slot timing checks, proposer signature verification, parent block validation, fork choice ancestry check, Merkle inclusion proof verification, KZG proof verification, deduplication, and proposer schedule verification. Subnet routing is only the first filter in a defense-in-depth chain.

actors.name: Blob Submitter (External Data Provider)
actors.description: Any entity that submits blob data to the system for encoding into polynomial commitments and cells. The blob is raw bytes of exactly BYTES_PER_BLOB length. This is the outermost data origin and the system's primary untrusted input surface. Blob content determines all downstream polynomial evaluations, KZG proofs, and data columns.

actors.name: KZG Trusted Setup (KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL / KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL)
actors.description: The cryptographic trusted setup ceremony that produced the structured reference string (SRS) used in KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL. This is a foundational trust anchor: all KZG proof generation (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl) and batch verification (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl) depend on these parameters being honestly generated. If the toxic waste from the ceremony was not destroyed, the entire commitment scheme is broken.

actors.name: Polynomial Arithmetic Engine
actors.description: The internal computational module implementing polynomial operations over BLS12-381 scalar fields: FFT (SG-003, SG-004), coset FFT (SG-005), polynomial addition (SG-008), multiplication (SG-009), division (SG-010), interpolation (SG-011), vanishing polynomial construction (SG-012), and Horner evaluation (SG-013). This engine is trusted as a correct implementation of finite field arithmetic.

actors.name: Cell Encoder / Decoder
actors.description: The component responsible for serializing and deserializing field elements to/from cell byte representations (SG-001 cell_to_coset_evals, SG-002 coset_evals_to_cell). It bridges the byte-level blob representation and the algebraic field element domain. Encoding/decoding correctness is critical: any error here corrupts all downstream polynomial operations.

actors.name: KZG Proof Generator
actors.description: The component that generates KZG multi-opening proofs for cells (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl, SG-019 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff, SG-020 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It computes quotient polynomials and commits them using the trusted setup. The proof generator must produce proofs that are verifiable by any honest verifier.

actors.name: Batch KZG Proof Verifier
actors.description: The component that performs batched verification of multiple cell KZG proofs in a single pairing check (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl, SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch). It uses the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006) to derive a random challenge for aggregation. This is the primary cryptographic gatekeeper: it determines whether cells and proofs are accepted as valid.

actors.name: Data Recovery Engine (Reed-Solomon Erasure Coding)
actors.description: The component that recovers the original polynomial from a subset of cells using Reed-Solomon erasure coding techniques (SG-023 recover_polynomialcoeff, SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It requires at least 50% of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2) to be available. Recovery correctness is essential for data availability guarantees.

actors.name: Fiat-Shamir Random Oracle (Hash Function)
actors.description: The hash function (hash_to_bls_field) used to derive the random challenge r for batch verification via the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006 compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge). This transforms the interactive proof protocol into a non-interactive one. The hash function is assumed to behave as a random oracle.

actors.name: Validator Node (Honest Validator)
actors.description: A consensus-layer validator that computes cells from blobs (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), constructs data column sidecars (SG-003 get_data_column_sidecars), publishes sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing), and retains sidecars for the required epochs (SG-007 sidecar_retention). Validators have custody requirements proportional to their stake (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement).

actors.name: Beacon State (Consensus State)
actors.description: The beacon chain state that provides validator effective balances for custody requirement computation (SG-001), block headers for sidecar construction (SG-004), and KZG commitments for data integrity verification. The state is assumed to be the canonical finalized or justified state.

actors.name: Gossip Network (Data Column Subnets)
actors.description: The GossipSub network layer over which data column sidecars are published (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is published to a deterministic subnet based on column_index. The network is an untrusted transport: messages may be dropped, duplicated, reordered, or injected by adversaries.

actors.name: Data Requester (Peer / Light Client)
actors.description: Any entity requesting data column sidecars from a validator during the retention period (SG-007 sidecar_retention). The validator MUST serve sidecars when requested during the retention period. The requester is untrusted and may attempt to exhaust the validator's resources.

trust_boundaries.description: Raw blob bytes cross into the cryptographic domain when cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001) converts byte segments into BLS field elements via bytes_to_bls_field. Malformed or adversarial blob data could encode values outside the valid field range. The assertion len(blob) == BYTES_PER_BLOB (SG-018 INV-001, SG-020 INV-002) is the first validation gate.

Deserialized field elements from cells are passed to the polynomial engine for FFT, interpolation, and evaluation. The polynomial engine trusts that input field elements are valid members of the BLS12-381 scalar field. If bytes_to_bls_field produces invalid field elements, all polynomial operations produce incorrect results.

Polynomial coefficients produced by polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007) are consumed by compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl (SG-014) to generate KZG proofs. The proof generator trusts the polynomial engine to produce correct coefficient representations. An incorrect inverse FFT or bit-reversal permutation would produce invalid proofs that fail verification.

The proof generator uses KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL from the trusted setup to compute g1_lincomb (SG-014, q_commit). The entire security of the KZG commitment scheme rests on the trusted setup being honestly generated. This is a static, ceremony-level trust boundary that cannot be verified at runtime.

The batch verifier uses both KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL[:n] and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL[n] from the trusted setup in the pairing check (SG-015). If the structured reference string is compromised, an adversary can forge proofs that pass batch verification for arbitrary data.

The batch verifier derives the random challenge r from the hash function via compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006). The challenge r must be unpredictable before all inputs are committed. The Fiat-Shamir heuristic security requires the hash function to behave as a random oracle and that all verifier-relevant data is included in the hash input (SG-006 INV-001).

When externally supplied cells, commitments, and proofs are presented for batch verification (SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch), all inputs cross from the untrusted external domain into the verifier. The verifier performs exhaustive input validation: length checks (INV-002), commitment byte length (BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT), cell index bounds (INV-004), cell byte length (BYTES_PER_CELL), and proof byte length (BYTES_PER_PROOF).

Partial cell sets provided for recovery (SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs) cross into the recovery engine. The recovery engine validates: matching lengths (INV-001), at least 50% cell availability (INV-002), no duplicate indices (INV-003), sorted indices (INV-003), valid index bounds (INV-004), and correct cell byte lengths. A malicious requester could provide inconsistent cells that lead to recovery of an incorrect polynomial.

The recovered polynomial from recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023) is passed to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff (SG-019) to regenerate all cells and proofs. If the recovery engine produces an incorrect polynomial (e.g., due to adversarial cell inputs or insufficient cell count), all regenerated proofs will be invalid.

Validators publish data column sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is deterministically routed to subnet compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar(column_index). The validator trusts the network to propagate sidecars, but the network may drop, censor, or delay them. Publishing to the wrong subnet would prevent peers from receiving the data.

The beacon state provides validator effective balances used to compute custody requirements (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement). Custody is proportional to stake: total_node_balance // BALANCE_PER_ADDITIONAL_CUSTODY_GROUP, clamped to [VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT, NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS]. The validator trusts the state to be canonical. A stale or forked state would produce incorrect custody assignments.

During the retention period (MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS), validators MUST serve sidecars on request (SG-007). Requesters are untrusted: they may flood the validator with requests (DoS), request data outside the retention window, or attempt to extract custody group membership information.

During sidecar construction (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), the validator uses compute_cells to decompose blobs into cells and pairs them with proofs from blobs_bundle.proofs. The validator trusts that blobs_bundle.proofs are correctly aligned with cells: proofs[start:end] for each blob (INV-003). Misaligned proof slicing would produce sidecars with invalid cell-proof pairings.

get_data_column_sidecars_from_block (SG-004) extracts KZG commitments and computes a Merkle inclusion proof from the signed block. The block is trusted (already validated), but the Merkle proof computation is a cryptographic operation that must correctly target the blob_kzg_commitments field in the block body. An incorrect proof would cause sidecar rejection by peers.

assumptions.text: KZG Trusted Setup Integrity: The structured reference string (KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL, KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL) was honestly generated during the trusted setup ceremony and all toxic waste was destroyed. If the discrete log relationships between setup elements are known to an adversary, they can forge valid KZG proofs for arbitrary polynomials, breaking all data availability guarantees.

BLS12-381 Cryptographic Hardness: The security of all KZG commitments, proofs, and pairing-based verification relies on the computational hardness of the discrete logarithm problem in BLS12-381 G1/G2 groups and the bilinear Diffie-Hellman assumption in the pairing group.

Random Oracle Model for Fiat-Shamir: The hash function hash_to_bls_field used in compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006) behaves as a random oracle. This is essential for the Fiat-Shamir transformation that converts interactive proof verification into non-interactive batch verification. A weak hash function could allow an adversary to predict the challenge r and craft inputs that pass verification despite invalid proofs.

Domain Separator Uniqueness: RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN (SG-006 INV-002) is globally unique across all protocols using the same hash function. This prevents cross-protocol replay attacks where a valid Fiat-Shamir challenge from one protocol context is reused in another.

Field Element Serialization Correctness: bytes_to_bls_field and bls_field_to_bytes (used in SG-001, SG-002) are assumed to be correct inverse operations for valid field elements. If serialization is lossy or non-injective, round-trip encoding errors corrupt cell data.

Polynomial Arithmetic Correctness: FFT (SG-003, SG-004), coset FFT (SG-005), polynomial addition (SG-008), multiplication (SG-009), division (SG-010), interpolation (SG-011), and evaluation (SG-013) are all assumed to be correctly implemented over the BLS12-381 scalar field. Any bug in these operations would silently produce incorrect proofs or failed verifications.

Roots of Unity Correctness: compute_roots_of_unity produces primitive roots of unity of the correct order for the BLS12-381 scalar field. PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY (SG-005 INV-001) must be a genuine primitive root. Incorrect roots would make FFT and coset evaluations algebraically meaningless.

Bit-Reversal Permutation Consistency: bit_reversal_permutation is applied consistently in polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007), coset_shift_for_cell (SG-016), coset_for_cell (SG-017), and recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023). Any inconsistency in permutation application between encoding and decoding paths would produce misaligned evaluations.
