Reed-Solomon Recovery Threshold: Data recovery (SG-023, SG-024) requires at least 50% of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2). This is the information-theoretic minimum for a rate-1/2 Reed-Solomon code over the extension domain. Below this threshold, recovery is impossible and the system must treat the data as unavailable.

Honest Majority of Validators for Data Availability: The data availability sampling scheme assumes that at least 50% of cells are available across the network. This requires a sufficient number of honest validators with correct custody assignments to collectively hold enough cells for recovery.

Custody Requirement Proportionality: Higher-staked validators (larger effective_balance) are assigned more custody groups (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement). The assumption is that validators with more stake have proportionally more resources (bandwidth, storage) and stronger incentive alignment, so burdening them with more custody is both fair and enforceable.

Sidecar Retention Compliance: Validators MUST retain sidecars for MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS epochs (SG-007 INV-001) and MUST serve them on request during this period (INV-002). Non-compliance (premature pruning) degrades data availability but is not directly slashable.

Gossip Subnet Routing Integrity: Sidecars are published to deterministic subnets via compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar (SG-006 INV-002). The assumption is that the subnet mapping function is consistent across all nodes and that the gossip network faithfully routes messages within each subnet.

Non-Zero Divisor in Polynomial Division: divide_polynomialcoeff (SG-010 INV-001) assumes the divisor polynomial b is not the zero polynomial, and that the leading coefficient b[bpos] is nonzero (INV-003). Division by zero would cause a runtime error or undefined behavior.

Lagrange Interpolation Point Distinctness: interpolate_polynomialcoeff (SG-011 INV-001) requires all x-values to be distinct for the weight_adjustment inverse (xs[i] - xs[j]).inverse() to exist. Duplicate x-values would cause division by zero in the field, crashing or producing incorrect interpolation.

Pairing Check Soundness: The bilinear pairing check e(LL, LR) == e(RL, [1]) in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl (SG-015 INV-001) is assumed to be sound: it rejects with overwhelming probability when the underlying KZG polynomial commitment relationship does not hold.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary submitting a blob with carefully crafted byte sequences that, when deserialized by bytes_to_bls_field, produce field elements that exploit edge cases in polynomial arithmetic (e.g., zero field elements that cause vanishing polynomial issues, or elements near the field modulus BLS_MODULUS that trigger overflow in non-modular implementations).
stride_analysis.mitigation: bytes_to_bls_field must validate that input bytes represent a value strictly less than BLS_MODULUS. The assertion len(blob) == BYTES_PER_BLOB and the fixed segment size of BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT (SG-001 INV-001) prevent buffer overflows.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit maximally-sized blobs (BYTES_PER_BLOB) that are computationally expensive to encode into cells, requiring a full inverse FFT (SG-007), CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB polynomial evaluations (SG-018), and CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB KZG proof generations (SG-019). The O(n^2) complexity noted in SG-019 INV-003 makes this a realistic resource exhaustion vector.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting blob submissions. The FK20 algorithm is recommended for production (SG-019 INV-003) to reduce proof generation complexity. Cell computation can be parallelized per-coset.

stride_analysis.description: If the KZG trusted setup is compromised (toxic waste not destroyed), an adversary possessing the secret trapdoor can forge KZG proofs that validate against arbitrary commitments. This would allow spoofing of data availability: a blob could appear committed and provable while the underlying data is garbage or unavailable.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Multi-party computation ceremonies with hundreds/thousands of participants where only one honest participant is needed. The trusted setup is a one-time operation and its integrity is the foundational security assumption of the entire PCS scheme.

stride_analysis.description: If KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL[n] used in the pairing check (SG-015 q_lr) is tampered with or does not correspond to the same trusted setup as KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL, the verification equation e(LL, LR) == e(RL, [1]) could accept invalid proofs or reject valid ones.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The G1 and G2 setup elements must be loaded from the same verified trusted setup file and validated against known checksums before use. Runtime consistency checks between G1 and G2 elements can detect mismatches.

stride_analysis.description: If an adversary can manipulate the hash input to compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006) by injecting, reordering, or omitting data before hashing, they could influence the random challenge r to make the batched verification equation hold for invalid proofs. The Fiat-Shamir challenge must bind to ALL verifier-relevant data.
stride_analysis.mitigation: SG-006 INV-001 mandates that all verifier-relevant data is included in the hash input. INV-003 requires the hash input order to be deterministic and canonical. The domain separator (INV-002) prevents cross-protocol manipulation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could attempt to find two different sets of (commitments, cells, proofs) that produce the same Fiat-Shamir challenge r, allowing them to substitute invalid data that passes batch verification. This is a collision attack on the hash function.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Using a collision-resistant hash function for hash_to_bls_field. The domain separator RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN ensures domain separation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit cells with valid formatting (correct BYTES_PER_CELL length, valid cell_indices) but containing tampered evaluation data that does not correspond to the claimed KZG commitment. The batch verifier must cryptographically reject such inputs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The pairing-based verification equation (SG-015 INV-001) cryptographically binds cells to commitments via KZG proofs. Random linear combination via the Fiat-Shamir challenge ensures that tampering with any individual cell is detected with overwhelming probability.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit a large batch of (commitments, cell_indices, cells, proofs) for verification, causing the verifier to perform expensive operations: multiple polynomial interpolations (SG-011 inside SG-015), g1_lincomb operations, and a final pairing check. The number of operations scales linearly with num_cells.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting verification requests. The batch verification itself (single pairing check for all cells, SG-015 INV-005) is already an optimization over individual verification. Maximum batch sizes should be bounded by protocol constants.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could present a valid proof for one (commitment, cell) pair and attempt to reuse it for a different cell or commitment. The Fiat-Shamir challenge computation includes all commitments, cell indices, cell evaluations, and proofs (SG-006), making such reuse detectable.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The deterministic inclusion of commitment_indices, cell_indices, coset evaluations, and proofs in the Fiat-Shamir hash (SG-006) ensures that any substitution changes the challenge r and invalidates the verification equation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary providing partial cells for recovery (SG-024) could supply cells that are individually valid but mutually inconsistent (e.g., cells from different polynomials). The recovery algorithm (SG-023) would produce a polynomial that differs from the original, leading to regenerated cells and proofs that fail verification against the original commitment.
stride_analysis.mitigation: After recovery, the regenerated cells and proofs should be verified against the original KZG commitment using verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021). This post-recovery verification step detects inconsistent input cells.

stride_analysis.description: Recovery (SG-023 recover_polynomialcoeff) involves multiple FFTs, inverse FFTs, coset FFTs, and element-wise operations over vectors of size FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB. An adversary could trigger recovery with the minimum number of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2) to maximize the size of the vanishing polynomial and thus computational cost.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Recovery is an inherently expensive operation. Nodes should rate-limit recovery attempts and prioritize recovering data for blocks that are on the canonical chain.

stride_analysis.description: The division step in recovery (SG-023 q_divide: reconstructed = [a / b for a, b in zip(ez_over_coset, zp_over_coset)]) could fail if zp_over_coset contains zero elements. While the coset FFT is specifically designed to avoid this (SG-005 INV-003), a failure would leak information about which cells were missing and potentially reveal side-channel timing information.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The coset FFT evaluation domain (using PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY as shift_factor) is specifically chosen to avoid evaluation points where the vanishing polynomial is zero (SG-005 INV-003). This is an algebraic guarantee, not a runtime check.

stride_analysis.description: If recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023) returns an incorrect polynomial (e.g., degree >= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB due to adversarial cells), the subsequent call to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff (SG-019) would generate cells and proofs for a different polynomial than the original blob. The truncation to FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB coefficients (SG-023 INV-003) is the boundary between the extended and original domain.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The recovered polynomial is truncated to degree < FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB (SG-023 INV-003). Post-recovery, cells and proofs should be verified against known KZG commitments. High-degree coefficients in the recovered polynomial (beyond FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB) should be verified to be zero.

stride_analysis.description: A network-level adversary could selectively drop or delay data column sidecars published to specific gossip subnets, preventing certain columns from reaching peers and degrading data availability below the 50% recovery threshold.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The custody group system distributes columns across multiple subnets and multiple validators. Redundancy in custody assignments ensures that even if some subnet paths are disrupted, sufficient cells remain available for recovery.

stride_analysis.description: A validator could claim to have published sidecars to the gossip network but actually withhold them (data withholding attack). Since gossip publication is not cryptographically logged, there is no proof of publication.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Data availability sampling by multiple independent peers detects withheld data. The economic incentive (staked ETH) aligns validators with honest behavior. Peer reputation scoring in GossipSub penalizes nodes that fail to propagate expected data.

stride_analysis.description: If the beacon state is forked or stale, the effective_balance values used in get_validators_custody_requirement (SG-001) would be incorrect, leading to validators being assigned too few or too many custody groups. Incorrect custody assignments could create coverage gaps where some columns are not held by any validator.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Custody requirements should be computed from the finalized or justified state to prevent fork-based manipulation. VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT (minimum 8) provides a floor that ensures every validator stores at least a baseline number of columns.

stride_analysis.description: A validator with low effective_balance could be assigned the minimum custody requirement (VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT), storing fewer columns. If many low-stake validators coordinate, they could collectively reduce the network's data availability coverage for specific columns.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The minimum floor VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT ensures every validator contributes meaningfully. The maximum cap NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS prevents any single validator from being overwhelmed. The proportional scaling via BALANCE_PER_ADDITIONAL_CUSTODY_GROUP ensures that the total custody across all validators provides sufficient redundancy.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could flood a validator with sidecar requests during the retention period (SG-007), exhausting its bandwidth, CPU, or I/O resources. The MUST serve requirement (INV-002) obligates the validator to respond, making this a protocol-level amplification vector.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting per-peer requests. Prioritizing requests from peers with known custody obligations. Limiting the maximum number of concurrent responses.

stride_analysis.description: By probing which columns a validator serves, an adversary could infer the validator's custody group membership, which reveals information about the validator's position in the shuffling algorithm and potentially its identity across epochs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Validators could serve all columns they have (not just custody columns) to mask their custody assignment. Response timing should not differ based on whether a column is in the validator's custody set.

stride_analysis.description: During cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction (SG-002), the proof slicing blobs_bundle.proofs[start:end] must be correctly aligned with the cells from compute_cells(blob). If blobs_bundle.proofs has an incorrect length or ordering, cell-proof pairs will be mismatched, producing invalid sidecars that peers will reject.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Invariant INV-002 (blobs_bundle.proofs has length CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB * len(blobs_bundle.blobs)) and INV-003 (slicing indices in bounds) provide structural correctness guarantees. The proof ordering must match the cell ordering from compute_cells.

stride_analysis.description: An incorrect Merkle inclusion proof computed in get_data_column_sidecars_from_block (SG-004) would cause all resulting sidecars to fail inclusion proof verification at receiving peers (the gossip validation step). This could be caused by targeting the wrong field index in the block body tree.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The Merkle proof must target the blob_kzg_commitments field at its correct generalized index in the BeaconBlockBody SSZ tree. The proof depth and leaf index must match the SSZ container specification for the Fulu fork.

stride_analysis.description: If polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007) does not correctly apply the inverse FFT with bit-reversal permutation, the resulting coefficient representation could have higher degree than expected. When passed to compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl (SG-014), the quotient polynomial could exceed the trusted setup bounds, potentially allowing the proof generator to produce commitments to polynomials outside the supported degree.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-001 of SG-007 constrains the input to exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB evaluations. INV-002 requires bit-reversal before inverse FFT. The assertion in multiply_polynomialcoeff (SG-009 INV-001: len(a) + len(b) <= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB) provides an explicit degree bound check.

stride_analysis.description: The recursive FFT implementation (SG-003 _fft_field) requires len(vals) to be a power of 2 (INV-001). Providing input of non-power-of-2 length would cause incorrect splits in the recursive calls, potentially leading to infinite recursion or incorrect results.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-001 of SG-003 mandates power-of-2 input length. INV-002 requires len(vals) == len(roots_of_unity). These invariants should be asserted at the entry point. All protocol-defined constants (FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB, FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB, FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL) are powers of 2.

actors.name: Blob Submitter (External Data Provider)
actors.description: Any entity that submits blob data to the system for encoding into polynomial commitments and cells. The blob is raw bytes of exactly BYTES_PER_BLOB length. This is the outermost data origin and the system's primary untrusted input surface. Blob content determines all downstream polynomial evaluations, KZG proofs, and data columns.

actors.name: KZG Trusted Setup (KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL / KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL)
actors.description: The cryptographic trusted setup ceremony that produced the structured reference string (SRS) used in KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL. This is a foundational trust anchor: all KZG proof generation (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl) and batch verification (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl) depend on these parameters being honestly generated. If the toxic waste from the ceremony was not destroyed, the entire commitment scheme is broken.

actors.name: Polynomial Arithmetic Engine
actors.description: The internal computational module implementing polynomial operations over BLS12-381 scalar fields: FFT (SG-003, SG-004), coset FFT (SG-005), polynomial addition (SG-008), multiplication (SG-009), division (SG-010), interpolation (SG-011), vanishing polynomial construction (SG-012), and Horner evaluation (SG-013). This engine is trusted as a correct implementation of finite field arithmetic.

actors.name: Cell Encoder / Decoder
actors.description: The component responsible for serializing and deserializing field elements to/from cell byte representations (SG-001 cell_to_coset_evals, SG-002 coset_evals_to_cell). It bridges the byte-level blob representation and the algebraic field element domain. Encoding/decoding correctness is critical: any error here corrupts all downstream polynomial operations.

actors.name: KZG Proof Generator
actors.description: The component that generates KZG multi-opening proofs for cells (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl, SG-019 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff, SG-020 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It computes quotient polynomials and commits them using the trusted setup. The proof generator must produce proofs that are verifiable by any honest verifier.

actors.name: Batch KZG Proof Verifier
actors.description: The component that performs batched verification of multiple cell KZG proofs in a single pairing check (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl, SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch). It uses the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006) to derive a random challenge for aggregation. This is the primary cryptographic gatekeeper: it determines whether cells and proofs are accepted as valid.

actors.name: Data Recovery Engine (Reed-Solomon Erasure Coding)
actors.description: The component that recovers the original polynomial from a subset of cells using Reed-Solomon erasure coding techniques (SG-023 recover_polynomialcoeff, SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It requires at least 50% of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2) to be available. Recovery correctness is essential for data availability guarantees.

actors.name: Fiat-Shamir Random Oracle (Hash Function)
actors.description: The hash function (hash_to_bls_field) used to derive the random challenge r for batch verification via the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006 compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge). This transforms the interactive proof protocol into a non-interactive one. The hash function is assumed to behave as a random oracle.

actors.name: Validator Node (Honest Validator)
actors.description: A consensus-layer validator that computes cells from blobs (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), constructs data column sidecars (SG-003 get_data_column_sidecars), publishes sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing), and retains sidecars for the required epochs (SG-007 sidecar_retention). Validators have custody requirements proportional to their stake (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement).

actors.name: Beacon State (Consensus State)
actors.description: The beacon chain state that provides validator effective balances for custody requirement computation (SG-001), block headers for sidecar construction (SG-004), and KZG commitments for data integrity verification. The state is assumed to be the canonical finalized or justified state.

actors.name: Gossip Network (Data Column Subnets)
actors.description: The GossipSub network layer over which data column sidecars are published (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is published to a deterministic subnet based on column_index. The network is an untrusted transport: messages may be dropped, duplicated, reordered, or injected by adversaries.

actors.name: Data Requester (Peer / Light Client)
actors.description: Any entity requesting data column sidecars from a validator during the retention period (SG-007 sidecar_retention). The validator MUST serve sidecars when requested during the retention period. The requester is untrusted and may attempt to exhaust the validator's resources.

actors.name: External User / Transaction Sender
actors.description: An external entity (EOA) that initiates transactions, including set-code transactions (EIP-7702) containing Authorization tuples. They originate the data that eventually gets encoded, hashed, and stored on-chain. Users interact with the deposit contract to make beacon chain deposits.

actors.name: Deposit Contract
actors.description: The canonical beacon chain deposit contract at address 0x00000000219ab540356cbb839cbe05303d7705fa. It emits DepositEvent logs that the execution layer parses to construct deposit requests for the consensus layer. Its address and event signature hash are hardcoded constants.

actors.name: Execution Layer Client
actors.description: The execution layer node software that processes blocks, executes transactions, maintains state tries, encodes accounts via RLP (encode_account), parses transaction receipts, extracts deposit data (extract_deposit_data, parse_deposit_requests), and computes requests hashes (compute_requests_hash). This is the system under specification for the Osaka fork types and requests modules.

actors.name: Consensus Layer
actors.description: The beacon chain / consensus layer that consumes the requests_hash and deposit requests produced by the execution layer. It relies on the execution layer to faithfully extract and order deposit data. It also performs BLS signature validation for deposits.

actors.name: Block Builder / Proposer
actors.description: The entity responsible for constructing blocks, ordering transactions, and producing the BlockOutput (including receipts_trie and receipt_keys). Controls transaction inclusion and ordering within a block, which directly determines deposit request ordering.

actors.name: State / Receipts Trie
actors.description: The Merkle Patricia Trie data structures (state trie for accounts, receipts trie for transaction receipts). These are trusted internal data structures that provide integrity guarantees via cryptographic roots.

actors.name: EIP-7702 Authorizer
actors.description: An account holder who signs an Authorization tuple (chain_id, address, nonce, y_parity, r, s) to delegate code execution to another address via EIP-7702 set-code transactions. This is a distinct role from the transaction sender.

trust_boundaries.description: Raw blob bytes cross into the cryptographic domain when cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001) converts byte segments into BLS field elements via bytes_to_bls_field. Malformed or adversarial blob data could encode values outside the valid field range. The assertion len(blob) == BYTES_PER_BLOB (SG-018 INV-001, SG-020 INV-002) is the first validation gate.

Deserialized field elements from cells are passed to the polynomial engine for FFT, interpolation, and evaluation. The polynomial engine trusts that input field elements are valid members of the BLS12-381 scalar field. If bytes_to_bls_field produces invalid field elements, all polynomial operations produce incorrect results.

Polynomial coefficients produced by polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007) are consumed by compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl (SG-014) to generate KZG proofs. The proof generator trusts the polynomial engine to produce correct coefficient representations. An incorrect inverse FFT or bit-reversal permutation would produce invalid proofs that fail verification.

The proof generator uses KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL from the trusted setup to compute g1_lincomb (SG-014, q_commit). The entire security of the KZG commitment scheme rests on the trusted setup being honestly generated. This is a static, ceremony-level trust boundary that cannot be verified at runtime.

The batch verifier uses both KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL[:n] and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL[n] from the trusted setup in the pairing check (SG-015). If the structured reference string is compromised, an adversary can forge proofs that pass batch verification for arbitrary data.

The batch verifier derives the random challenge r from the hash function via compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006). The challenge r must be unpredictable before all inputs are committed. The Fiat-Shamir heuristic security requires the hash function to behave as a random oracle and that all verifier-relevant data is included in the hash input (SG-006 INV-001).

When externally supplied cells, commitments, and proofs are presented for batch verification (SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch), all inputs cross from the untrusted external domain into the verifier. The verifier performs exhaustive input validation: length checks (INV-002), commitment byte length (BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT), cell index bounds (INV-004), cell byte length (BYTES_PER_CELL), and proof byte length (BYTES_PER_PROOF).

Partial cell sets provided for recovery (SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs) cross into the recovery engine. The recovery engine validates: matching lengths (INV-001), at least 50% cell availability (INV-002), no duplicate indices (INV-003), sorted indices (INV-003), valid index bounds (INV-004), and correct cell byte lengths. A malicious requester could provide inconsistent cells that lead to recovery of an incorrect polynomial.

The recovered polynomial from recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023) is passed to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff (SG-019) to regenerate all cells and proofs. If the recovery engine produces an incorrect polynomial (e.g., due to adversarial cell inputs or insufficient cell count), all regenerated proofs will be invalid.

Validators publish data column sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is deterministically routed to subnet compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar(column_index). The validator trusts the network to propagate sidecars, but the network may drop, censor, or delay them. Publishing to the wrong subnet would prevent peers from receiving the data.

The beacon state provides validator effective balances used to compute custody requirements (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement). Custody is proportional to stake: total_node_balance // BALANCE_PER_ADDITIONAL_CUSTODY_GROUP, clamped to [VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT, NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS]. The validator trusts the state to be canonical. A stale or forked state would produce incorrect custody assignments.

During the retention period (MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS), validators MUST serve sidecars on request (SG-007). Requesters are untrusted: they may flood the validator with requests (DoS), request data outside the retention window, or attempt to extract custody group membership information.

During sidecar construction (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), the validator uses compute_cells to decompose blobs into cells and pairs them with proofs from blobs_bundle.proofs. The validator trusts that blobs_bundle.proofs are correctly aligned with cells: proofs[start:end] for each blob (INV-003). Misaligned proof slicing would produce sidecars with invalid cell-proof pairings.

get_data_column_sidecars_from_block (SG-004) extracts KZG commitments and computes a Merkle inclusion proof from the signed block. The block is trusted (already validated), but the Merkle proof computation is a cryptographic operation that must correctly target the blob_kzg_commitments field in the block body. An incorrect proof would cause sidecar rejection by peers.

External user submits transaction data (including account data and EIP-7702 Authorization tuples) to the execution client. The execution client must validate all inputs before processing. This is the primary untrusted input boundary for the execution layer.

The deposit contract emits DepositEvent logs that the execution client must parse. The execution client trusts the log data format but validates all offsets and sizes rigorously via extract_deposit_data. The contract address (DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS) and event signature hash (DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH) serve as the authentication mechanism.

The execution client produces deposit_requests bytes and a requests_hash (via compute_requests_hash using SHA-256) that the consensus layer consumes. The consensus layer trusts the execution client to have faithfully extracted and ordered all deposit data from transaction receipts.

The block builder provides the BlockOutput containing receipt_keys ordering and the receipts_trie. The execution client trusts receipt_keys ordering to determine deposit request ordering, and trusts that trie_get returns valid receipt data for each key.

An EIP-7702 authorizer signs an Authorization tuple with ECDSA (y_parity, r, s) delegating code to a target address. The execution client must verify the signature, chain_id for replay protection, and nonce for freshness before accepting the authorization.

The execution client reads from and writes to the state and receipts tries. Account encoding (encode_account) produces RLP data stored in the state trie. The storage_root parameter is a 32-byte Merkle root that must be externally supplied and correct. The function does not validate this value.

External users interact with the deposit contract to make beacon chain deposits. The contract emits logs that later get parsed by the execution client. The user trusts the contract to correctly emit deposit events with proper BLS public keys, withdrawal credentials, amounts, signatures, and indices.

assumptions.text: KZG Trusted Setup Integrity: The structured reference string (KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL, KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL) was honestly generated during the trusted setup ceremony and all toxic waste was destroyed. If the discrete log relationships between setup elements are known to an adversary, they can forge valid KZG proofs for arbitrary polynomials, breaking all data availability guarantees.

BLS12-381 Cryptographic Hardness: The security of all KZG commitments, proofs, and pairing-based verification relies on the computational hardness of the discrete logarithm problem in BLS12-381 G1/G2 groups and the bilinear Diffie-Hellman assumption in the pairing group.

Random Oracle Model for Fiat-Shamir: The hash function hash_to_bls_field used in compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006) behaves as a random oracle. This is essential for the Fiat-Shamir transformation that converts interactive proof verification into non-interactive batch verification. A weak hash function could allow an adversary to predict the challenge r and craft inputs that pass verification despite invalid proofs.

Domain Separator Uniqueness: RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN (SG-006 INV-002) is globally unique across all protocols using the same hash function. This prevents cross-protocol replay attacks where a valid Fiat-Shamir challenge from one protocol context is reused in another.

Field Element Serialization Correctness: bytes_to_bls_field and bls_field_to_bytes (used in SG-001, SG-002) are assumed to be correct inverse operations for valid field elements. If serialization is lossy or non-injective, round-trip encoding errors corrupt cell data.

Polynomial Arithmetic Correctness: FFT (SG-003, SG-004), coset FFT (SG-005), polynomial addition (SG-008), multiplication (SG-009), division (SG-010), interpolation (SG-011), and evaluation (SG-013) are all assumed to be correctly implemented over the BLS12-381 scalar field. Any bug in these operations would silently produce incorrect proofs or failed verifications.

Roots of Unity Correctness: compute_roots_of_unity produces primitive roots of unity of the correct order for the BLS12-381 scalar field. PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY (SG-005 INV-001) must be a genuine primitive root. Incorrect roots would make FFT and coset evaluations algebraically meaningless.

Bit-Reversal Permutation Consistency: bit_reversal_permutation is applied consistently in polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007), coset_shift_for_cell (SG-016), coset_for_cell (SG-017), and recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023). Any inconsistency in permutation application between encoding and decoding paths would produce misaligned evaluations.

Reed-Solomon Recovery Threshold: Data recovery (SG-023, SG-024) requires at least 50% of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2). This is the information-theoretic minimum for a rate-1/2 Reed-Solomon code over the extension domain. Below this threshold, recovery is impossible and the system must treat the data as unavailable.

Honest Majority of Validators for Data Availability: The data availability sampling scheme assumes that at least 50% of cells are available across the network. This requires a sufficient number of honest validators with correct custody assignments to collectively hold enough cells for recovery.

Custody Requirement Proportionality: Higher-staked validators (larger effective_balance) are assigned more custody groups (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement). The assumption is that validators with more stake have proportionally more resources (bandwidth, storage) and stronger incentive alignment, so burdening them with more custody is both fair and enforceable.

Sidecar Retention Compliance: Validators MUST retain sidecars for MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS epochs (SG-007 INV-001) and MUST serve them on request during this period (INV-002). Non-compliance (premature pruning) degrades data availability but is not directly slashable.

Gossip Subnet Routing Integrity: Sidecars are published to deterministic subnets via compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar (SG-006 INV-002). The assumption is that the subnet mapping function is consistent across all nodes and that the gossip network faithfully routes messages within each subnet.

Non-Zero Divisor in Polynomial Division: divide_polynomialcoeff (SG-010 INV-001) assumes the divisor polynomial b is not the zero polynomial, and that the leading coefficient b[bpos] is nonzero (INV-003). Division by zero would cause a runtime error or undefined behavior.

Lagrange Interpolation Point Distinctness: interpolate_polynomialcoeff (SG-011 INV-001) requires all x-values to be distinct for the weight_adjustment inverse (xs[i] - xs[j]).inverse() to exist. Duplicate x-values would cause division by zero in the field, crashing or producing incorrect interpolation.

Pairing Check Soundness: The bilinear pairing check e(LL, LR) == e(RL, [1]) in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl (SG-015 INV-001) is assumed to be sound: it rejects with overwhelming probability when the underlying KZG polynomial commitment relationship does not hold.

Deposit Contract Correctness and Immutability: The deposit contract at DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS (0x00000000219ab540356cbb839cbe05303d7705fa) is assumed to be correct and immutable. Its address is hardcoded, and all deposit event parsing depends on this address being the legitimate deposit contract.

Deposit Event Signature Hash Correctness: The DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH (0x649bbc62d0e31342afea4e5cd82d4049e7e1ee912fc0889aa790803be39038c5) is assumed to be the correct keccak256 hash of the DepositEvent signature. Any change to the event signature would silently cause deposits to be missed.

Deposit Event Data Format Rigidity: The deposit event data format has fixed, known offsets and sizes (PUBKEY_OFFSET=160, WC_OFFSET=256, AMOUNT_OFFSET=320, SIGNATURE_OFFSET=384, INDEX_OFFSET=512) with total length of exactly 576 bytes. The extract_deposit_data function validates these rigorously; any deviation results in InvalidBlock.

Receipt Processing Order Determinism: Receipt processing order (block_output.receipt_keys) deterministically defines the deposit request ordering. All compliant execution clients must iterate receipt keys in the same order to produce identical deposit_requests bytes.

Receipts Trie Completeness: trie_get is assumed to always return a valid (non-None) receipt for every key in block_output.receipt_keys. A None return triggers an assertion failure, halting execution rather than silently skipping deposits.

Storage Root External Validity: The storage_root parameter passed to encode_account is assumed to be a valid 32-byte Merkle root of the account's storage trie. The encode_account function does not validate this value; it is the caller's responsibility to provide the correct root.

Hash Function Collision Resistance: keccak256 and SHA-256 hash functions are assumed to be collision-resistant and preimage-resistant. Account encoding relies on keccak256 of the code, and compute_requests_hash relies on SHA-256 of individual requests.

RLP Encoding Determinism: RLP encoding is assumed to be deterministic and canonical. The tuple ordering (nonce, balance, storage_root, code_hash) in encode_account must be strictly maintained for consensus compatibility.

Authorization Signature Verification Elsewhere: Authorization signatures (y_parity, r, s) in the Authorization dataclass are assumed to be verified elsewhere in the transaction processing pipeline. The dataclass itself only stores the signature components without performing verification.

EIP-7702 Replay Protection: The chain_id in Authorization provides replay protection across different chains. The nonce must match the current nonce of the authorizing account to prevent replay within the same chain.

Type Alias Length Enforcement: Type aliases (Address=Bytes20, Root=Hash32, VersionedHash=Hash32, Bloom=Bytes256) enforce length constraints at construction time. Any violation of these byte-length constraints would propagate errors through the system.

EMPTY_ACCOUNT Immutability: EMPTY_ACCOUNT is a module-level frozen constant that cannot be mutated after construction (via @slotted_freezable). Its immutability is critical for correctness as a sentinel value.

Requests Hash Construction: compute_requests_hash uses a two-level SHA-256 construction (hash each request individually, then feed digests into an outer hash). Order matters, and the double-hashing provides domain separation between individual requests.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary submitting a blob with carefully crafted byte sequences that, when deserialized by bytes_to_bls_field, produce field elements that exploit edge cases in polynomial arithmetic (e.g., zero field elements that cause vanishing polynomial issues, or elements near the field modulus BLS_MODULUS that trigger overflow in non-modular implementations).
stride_analysis.mitigation: bytes_to_bls_field must validate that input bytes represent a value strictly less than BLS_MODULUS. The assertion len(blob) == BYTES_PER_BLOB and the fixed segment size of BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT (SG-001 INV-001) prevent buffer overflows.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit maximally-sized blobs (BYTES_PER_BLOB) that are computationally expensive to encode into cells, requiring a full inverse FFT (SG-007), CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB polynomial evaluations (SG-018), and CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB KZG proof generations (SG-019). The O(n^2) complexity noted in SG-019 INV-003 makes this a realistic resource exhaustion vector.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting blob submissions. The FK20 algorithm is recommended for production (SG-019 INV-003) to reduce proof generation complexity. Cell computation can be parallelized per-coset.

stride_analysis.description: If the KZG trusted setup is compromised (toxic waste not destroyed), an adversary possessing the secret trapdoor can forge KZG proofs that validate against arbitrary commitments. This would allow spoofing of data availability: a blob could appear committed and provable while the underlying data is garbage or unavailable.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Multi-party computation ceremonies with hundreds/thousands of participants where only one honest participant is needed. The trusted setup is a one-time operation and its integrity is the foundational security assumption of the entire PCS scheme.

stride_analysis.description: If KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL[n] used in the pairing check (SG-015 q_lr) is tampered with or does not correspond to the same trusted setup as KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL, the verification equation e(LL, LR) == e(RL, [1]) could accept invalid proofs or reject valid ones.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The G1 and G2 setup elements must be loaded from the same verified trusted setup file and validated against known checksums before use. Runtime consistency checks between G1 and G2 elements can detect mismatches.

stride_analysis.description: If an adversary can manipulate the hash input to compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006) by injecting, reordering, or omitting data before hashing, they could influence the random challenge r to make the batched verification equation hold for invalid proofs. The Fiat-Shamir challenge must bind to ALL verifier-relevant data.
stride_analysis.mitigation: SG-006 INV-001 mandates that all verifier-relevant data is included in the hash input. INV-003 requires the hash input order to be deterministic and canonical. The domain separator (INV-002) prevents cross-protocol manipulation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could attempt to find two different sets of (commitments, cells, proofs) that produce the same Fiat-Shamir challenge r, allowing them to substitute invalid data that passes batch verification. This is a collision attack on the hash function.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Using a collision-resistant hash function for hash_to_bls_field. The domain separator RANDOM_CHALLENGE_KZG_CELL_BATCH_DOMAIN ensures domain separation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit cells with valid formatting (correct BYTES_PER_CELL length, valid cell_indices) but containing tampered evaluation data that does not correspond to the claimed KZG commitment. The batch verifier must cryptographically reject such inputs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The pairing-based verification equation (SG-015 INV-001) cryptographically binds cells to commitments via KZG proofs. Random linear combination via the Fiat-Shamir challenge ensures that tampering with any individual cell is detected with overwhelming probability.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could submit a large batch of (commitments, cell_indices, cells, proofs) for verification, causing the verifier to perform expensive operations: multiple polynomial interpolations (SG-011 inside SG-015), g1_lincomb operations, and a final pairing check. The number of operations scales linearly with num_cells.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting verification requests. The batch verification itself (single pairing check for all cells, SG-015 INV-005) is already an optimization over individual verification. Maximum batch sizes should be bounded by protocol constants.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could present a valid proof for one (commitment, cell) pair and attempt to reuse it for a different cell or commitment. The Fiat-Shamir challenge computation includes all commitments, cell indices, cell evaluations, and proofs (SG-006), making such reuse detectable.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The deterministic inclusion of commitment_indices, cell_indices, coset evaluations, and proofs in the Fiat-Shamir hash (SG-006) ensures that any substitution changes the challenge r and invalidates the verification equation.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary providing partial cells for recovery (SG-024) could supply cells that are individually valid but mutually inconsistent (e.g., cells from different polynomials). The recovery algorithm (SG-023) would produce a polynomial that differs from the original, leading to regenerated cells and proofs that fail verification against the original commitment.
stride_analysis.mitigation: After recovery, the regenerated cells and proofs should be verified against the original KZG commitment using verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021). This post-recovery verification step detects inconsistent input cells.

stride_analysis.description: Recovery (SG-023 recover_polynomialcoeff) involves multiple FFTs, inverse FFTs, coset FFTs, and element-wise operations over vectors of size FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB. An adversary could trigger recovery with the minimum number of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2) to maximize the size of the vanishing polynomial and thus computational cost.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Recovery is an inherently expensive operation. Nodes should rate-limit recovery attempts and prioritize recovering data for blocks that are on the canonical chain.

stride_analysis.description: The division step in recovery (SG-023 q_divide: reconstructed = [a / b for a, b in zip(ez_over_coset, zp_over_coset)]) could fail if zp_over_coset contains zero elements. While the coset FFT is specifically designed to avoid this (SG-005 INV-003), a failure would leak information about which cells were missing and potentially reveal side-channel timing information.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The coset FFT evaluation domain (using PRIMITIVE_ROOT_OF_UNITY as shift_factor) is specifically chosen to avoid evaluation points where the vanishing polynomial is zero (SG-005 INV-003). This is an algebraic guarantee, not a runtime check.

stride_analysis.description: If recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023) returns an incorrect polynomial (e.g., degree >= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB due to adversarial cells), the subsequent call to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff (SG-019) would generate cells and proofs for a different polynomial than the original blob. The truncation to FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB coefficients (SG-023 INV-003) is the boundary between the extended and original domain.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The recovered polynomial is truncated to degree < FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB (SG-023 INV-003). Post-recovery, cells and proofs should be verified against known KZG commitments. High-degree coefficients in the recovered polynomial (beyond FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB) should be verified to be zero.

stride_analysis.description: A network-level adversary could selectively drop or delay data column sidecars published to specific gossip subnets, preventing certain columns from reaching peers and degrading data availability below the 50% recovery threshold.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The custody group system distributes columns across multiple subnets and multiple validators. Redundancy in custody assignments ensures that even if some subnet paths are disrupted, sufficient cells remain available for recovery.

stride_analysis.description: A validator could claim to have published sidecars to the gossip network but actually withhold them (data withholding attack). Since gossip publication is not cryptographically logged, there is no proof of publication.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Data availability sampling by multiple independent peers detects withheld data. The economic incentive (staked ETH) aligns validators with honest behavior. Peer reputation scoring in GossipSub penalizes nodes that fail to propagate expected data.

stride_analysis.description: If the beacon state is forked or stale, the effective_balance values used in get_validators_custody_requirement (SG-001) would be incorrect, leading to validators being assigned too few or too many custody groups. Incorrect custody assignments could create coverage gaps where some columns are not held by any validator.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Custody requirements should be computed from the finalized or justified state to prevent fork-based manipulation. VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT (minimum 8) provides a floor that ensures every validator stores at least a baseline number of columns.

stride_analysis.description: A validator with low effective_balance could be assigned the minimum custody requirement (VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT), storing fewer columns. If many low-stake validators coordinate, they could collectively reduce the network's data availability coverage for specific columns.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The minimum floor VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT ensures every validator contributes meaningfully. The maximum cap NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS prevents any single validator from being overwhelmed. The proportional scaling via BALANCE_PER_ADDITIONAL_CUSTODY_GROUP ensures that the total custody across all validators provides sufficient redundancy.

stride_analysis.description: An adversary could flood a validator with sidecar requests during the retention period (SG-007), exhausting its bandwidth, CPU, or I/O resources. The MUST serve requirement (INV-002) obligates the validator to respond, making this a protocol-level amplification vector.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Rate-limiting per-peer requests. Prioritizing requests from peers with known custody obligations. Limiting the maximum number of concurrent responses.

stride_analysis.description: By probing which columns a validator serves, an adversary could infer the validator's custody group membership, which reveals information about the validator's position in the shuffling algorithm and potentially its identity across epochs.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Validators could serve all columns they have (not just custody columns) to mask their custody assignment. Response timing should not differ based on whether a column is in the validator's custody set.

stride_analysis.description: During cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction (SG-002), the proof slicing blobs_bundle.proofs[start:end] must be correctly aligned with the cells from compute_cells(blob). If blobs_bundle.proofs has an incorrect length or ordering, cell-proof pairs will be mismatched, producing invalid sidecars that peers will reject.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Invariant INV-002 (blobs_bundle.proofs has length CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB * len(blobs_bundle.blobs)) and INV-003 (slicing indices in bounds) provide structural correctness guarantees. The proof ordering must match the cell ordering from compute_cells.

stride_analysis.description: An incorrect Merkle inclusion proof computed in get_data_column_sidecars_from_block (SG-004) would cause all resulting sidecars to fail inclusion proof verification at receiving peers (the gossip validation step). This could be caused by targeting the wrong field index in the block body tree.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The Merkle proof must target the blob_kzg_commitments field at its correct generalized index in the BeaconBlockBody SSZ tree. The proof depth and leaf index must match the SSZ container specification for the Fulu fork.

stride_analysis.description: If polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007) does not correctly apply the inverse FFT with bit-reversal permutation, the resulting coefficient representation could have higher degree than expected. When passed to compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl (SG-014), the quotient polynomial could exceed the trusted setup bounds, potentially allowing the proof generator to produce commitments to polynomials outside the supported degree.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-001 of SG-007 constrains the input to exactly FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB evaluations. INV-002 requires bit-reversal before inverse FFT. The assertion in multiply_polynomialcoeff (SG-009 INV-001: len(a) + len(b) <= FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB) provides an explicit degree bound check.

stride_analysis.description: The recursive FFT implementation (SG-003 _fft_field) requires len(vals) to be a power of 2 (INV-001). Providing input of non-power-of-2 length would cause incorrect splits in the recursive calls, potentially leading to infinite recursion or incorrect results.
stride_analysis.mitigation: INV-001 of SG-003 mandates power-of-2 input length. INV-002 requires len(vals) == len(roots_of_unity). These invariants should be asserted at the entry point. All protocol-defined constants (FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_BLOB, FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_EXT_BLOB, FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL) are powers of 2.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could craft a transaction with a forged Authorization tuple, claiming to be a different account holder. The ECDSA signature (y_parity, r, s) in the Authorization struct is the primary defense, but the Authorization dataclass does not itself verify the signature.
stride_analysis.mitigation: ECDSA signature verification must be performed by the transaction processing pipeline before any Authorization is acted upon. The chain_id field provides cross-chain replay protection.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious actor could submit account data with manipulated nonce, balance, or code values. If these values are accepted without validation, encode_account would produce an incorrect RLP encoding, corrupting the state trie.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Account data must come from the validated state trie. The encode_account function is a pure encoder that trusts its inputs; the caller must ensure the Account dataclass is populated from verified state.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious contract could attempt to spoof deposit events by emitting logs that mimic the DepositEvent signature. If the contract address check is bypassed, fraudulent deposits could be injected into the consensus layer.
stride_analysis.mitigation: parse_deposit_requests performs a two-layer filter: (1) log.address must exactly equal DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, and (2) log.topics[0] must equal DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH. Only logs from the canonical deposit contract address are processed.

stride_analysis.description: A compromised or malicious deposit contract could emit logs with malformed data (incorrect offsets, sizes, or truncated data). This could cause incorrect deposit data extraction or crash the execution client.
stride_analysis.mitigation: extract_deposit_data performs exhaustive validation: it checks the total data length (576 bytes), validates all five field offsets against expected constants (PUBKEY_OFFSET, WITHDRAWAL_CREDENTIALS_OFFSET, AMOUNT_OFFSET, SIGNATURE_OFFSET, INDEX_OFFSET), and verifies all five field sizes (48, 32, 8, 96, 8). Any mismatch raises InvalidBlock, rejecting the entire block.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could flood the deposit contract with many small deposits, causing a large number of deposit logs in a single block. This increases processing time for parse_deposit_requests and inflates the deposit_requests byte array.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The deposit contract itself has a minimum deposit amount (1 ETH for mainnet). Gas costs for deposit transactions provide economic rate limiting. However, no explicit limit exists in the extraction code on the number of deposits per block.

stride_analysis.description: If the execution client produces an incorrect requests_hash (e.g., by omitting deposits, reordering them, or including phantom deposits), the consensus layer would accept a corrupted view of deposits.
stride_analysis.mitigation: compute_requests_hash uses SHA-256 to produce a commitment over all requests in order. Consensus nodes independently verify this hash, and any mismatch causes block rejection. The deterministic extraction process ensures all compliant clients produce the same output.

stride_analysis.description: An execution client could claim it processed deposits correctly while having silently dropped or altered some. Without the requests_hash commitment, there would be no way to verify completeness.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The requests_hash provides a cryptographic commitment to the exact set and order of all requests. Multiple independent execution clients can verify the same block and must arrive at the same hash, providing non-repudiation.

stride_analysis.description: Deposit data (public keys, withdrawal credentials, amounts, signatures) is inherently public as it originates from on-chain transaction logs. The aggregated deposit_requests byte stream reveals patterns about validator registrations.
stride_analysis.mitigation: This is by design. Beacon chain deposits are public operations. No sensitive data is disclosed beyond what is already visible in transaction logs.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious block builder could manipulate receipt_keys ordering to change the order of deposit requests, potentially affecting consensus layer processing. Since deposit ordering is deterministic based on receipt_keys, a different ordering would produce a different requests_hash.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Receipt keys ordering must follow the canonical transaction ordering within the block. The receipts_trie root is committed to in the block header, and consensus rules enforce deterministic receipt key ordering.

stride_analysis.description: A block builder could include transactions that produce a very large number of logs from the deposit contract, causing parse_deposit_requests to iterate over many receipts and logs, potentially slowing down block processing.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Block gas limits constrain the total number of transactions and thus the total number of logs. The deposit contract's gas cost per deposit provides an economic bound on the number of deposit events per block.

stride_analysis.description: If trie_get returns None for a key in receipt_keys (violating the assumption that all keys have valid receipts), the assertion failure (assert receipt is not None) would halt the client rather than gracefully handling the error. A crafted block could exploit this to crash execution clients.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The assert statement acts as a fail-stop mechanism. While this prevents silent data loss, it means a malformed block causes a crash rather than graceful rejection. Block validation at the consensus level should prevent such blocks from being accepted.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could attempt to create an Authorization tuple that impersonates another account by forging the ECDSA signature. If the signature verification is flawed or missing, unauthorized code delegation could occur, allowing the attacker to execute code in the victim's context.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The Authorization dataclass stores the full ECDSA signature components (y_parity, r, s). Signature verification must be performed by the EIP-7702 transaction processing logic, recovering the signer's address and confirming it matches the expected authorizer.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could replay an Authorization on a different chain if chain_id is not properly validated, or replay it on the same chain if the nonce check is bypassed. This would allow unauthorized code delegation using a previously valid authorization.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The chain_id field provides cross-chain replay protection (must match the target chain). The nonce field must match the current nonce of the authorizing account, preventing same-chain replay. Both checks must be enforced during transaction validation.

stride_analysis.description: EIP-7702 set-code transactions allow an EOA to delegate code execution to another address. If the authorization is accepted without proper nonce and chain_id validation, an attacker could gain the ability to execute arbitrary code in the context of the victim's account, effectively taking control of the account.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Strict validation of chain_id (must match current chain), nonce (must match authorizer's current nonce), and signature (must recover to the authorizer's address) prevents unauthorized code delegation.

stride_analysis.description: The storage_root passed to encode_account is not validated by the function itself. An incorrect storage_root would produce a valid-looking but semantically incorrect account encoding, corrupting the state trie and causing consensus divergence.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The storage_root must be computed from the account's actual storage trie by the caller. The Merkle Patricia Trie structure ensures that any tampering with storage entries changes the root, and consensus nodes independently verify state roots.

stride_analysis.description: The encode_account function hashes the account's code with keccak256 before storing it. Account balances and nonces are stored in plaintext in the RLP encoding, making them visible to anyone who reads the state trie.
stride_analysis.mitigation: This is by design in Ethereum's account model. All account state is public and verifiable. The code hash provides integrity without requiring storage of the full code in the account encoding.

stride_analysis.description: A user interacting with the deposit contract could submit deposits with invalid BLS public keys, withdrawal credentials, or signatures. The execution layer extracts this data faithfully without validating the cryptographic correctness of BLS signatures or public keys.
stride_analysis.mitigation: BLS signature validation for deposits is performed by the consensus layer (beacon chain), not the execution layer. The execution layer's role is limited to faithful extraction and forwarding. Invalid deposits are rejected at the consensus layer.

stride_analysis.description: A depositor could claim they did not make a deposit, or that their deposit was altered in transit. Without proper event logging and commitment, such claims would be difficult to refute.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Deposit events are recorded as immutable transaction logs on the execution layer. The requests_hash provides a cryptographic commitment to all deposits in a block. The BLS signature in the deposit data itself proves the depositor's intent.

actors.name: Blob Submitter (External Data Provider)
actors.description: Any entity that submits blob data to the system for encoding into polynomial commitments and cells. The blob is raw bytes of exactly BYTES_PER_BLOB length. This is the outermost data origin and the system's primary untrusted input surface. Blob content determines all downstream polynomial evaluations, KZG proofs, and data columns.

actors.name: KZG Trusted Setup (KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL / KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL)
actors.description: The cryptographic trusted setup ceremony that produced the structured reference string (SRS) used in KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL. This is a foundational trust anchor: all KZG proof generation (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl) and batch verification (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl) depend on these parameters being honestly generated. If the toxic waste from the ceremony was not destroyed, the entire commitment scheme is broken.

actors.name: Polynomial Arithmetic Engine
actors.description: The internal computational module implementing polynomial operations over BLS12-381 scalar fields: FFT (SG-003, SG-004), coset FFT (SG-005), polynomial addition (SG-008), multiplication (SG-009), division (SG-010), interpolation (SG-011), vanishing polynomial construction (SG-012), and Horner evaluation (SG-013). This engine is trusted as a correct implementation of finite field arithmetic.

actors.name: Cell Encoder / Decoder
actors.description: The component responsible for serializing and deserializing field elements to/from cell byte representations (SG-001 cell_to_coset_evals, SG-002 coset_evals_to_cell). It bridges the byte-level blob representation and the algebraic field element domain. Encoding/decoding correctness is critical: any error here corrupts all downstream polynomial operations.

actors.name: KZG Proof Generator
actors.description: The component that generates KZG multi-opening proofs for cells (SG-014 compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl, SG-019 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff, SG-020 compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It computes quotient polynomials and commits them using the trusted setup. The proof generator must produce proofs that are verifiable by any honest verifier.

actors.name: Batch KZG Proof Verifier
actors.description: The component that performs batched verification of multiple cell KZG proofs in a single pairing check (SG-015 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl, SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch). It uses the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006) to derive a random challenge for aggregation. This is the primary cryptographic gatekeeper: it determines whether cells and proofs are accepted as valid.

actors.name: Data Recovery Engine (Reed-Solomon Erasure Coding)
actors.description: The component that recovers the original polynomial from a subset of cells using Reed-Solomon erasure coding techniques (SG-023 recover_polynomialcoeff, SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs). It requires at least 50% of cells (CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB / 2) to be available. Recovery correctness is essential for data availability guarantees.

actors.name: Fiat-Shamir Random Oracle (Hash Function)
actors.description: The hash function (hash_to_bls_field) used to derive the random challenge r for batch verification via the Fiat-Shamir heuristic (SG-006 compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge). This transforms the interactive proof protocol into a non-interactive one. The hash function is assumed to behave as a random oracle.

actors.name: Validator Node (Honest Validator)
actors.description: A consensus-layer validator that computes cells from blobs (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), constructs data column sidecars (SG-003 get_data_column_sidecars), publishes sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing), and retains sidecars for the required epochs (SG-007 sidecar_retention). Validators have custody requirements proportional to their stake (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement).

actors.name: Beacon State (Consensus State)
actors.description: The beacon chain state that provides validator effective balances for custody requirement computation (SG-001), block headers for sidecar construction (SG-004), and KZG commitments for data integrity verification. The state is assumed to be the canonical finalized or justified state.

actors.name: Gossip Network (Data Column Subnets)
actors.description: The GossipSub network layer over which data column sidecars are published (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is published to a deterministic subnet based on column_index. The network is an untrusted transport: messages may be dropped, duplicated, reordered, or injected by adversaries.

actors.name: Data Requester (Peer / Light Client)
actors.description: Any entity requesting data column sidecars from a validator during the retention period (SG-007 sidecar_retention). The validator MUST serve sidecars when requested during the retention period. The requester is untrusted and may attempt to exhaust the validator's resources.

actors.name: External User / Transaction Sender
actors.description: An external entity (EOA) that initiates transactions, including set-code transactions (EIP-7702) containing Authorization tuples. They originate the data that eventually gets encoded, hashed, and stored on-chain. Users interact with the deposit contract to make beacon chain deposits.

actors.name: Deposit Contract
actors.description: The canonical beacon chain deposit contract at address 0x00000000219ab540356cbb839cbe05303d7705fa. It emits DepositEvent logs that the execution layer parses to construct deposit requests for the consensus layer. Its address and event signature hash are hardcoded constants.

actors.name: Execution Layer Client
actors.description: The execution layer node software that processes blocks, executes transactions, maintains state tries, encodes accounts via RLP (encode_account), parses transaction receipts, extracts deposit data (extract_deposit_data, parse_deposit_requests), computes requests hashes (compute_requests_hash), encodes and decodes receipts (encode_receipt, decode_receipt), constructs bloom filters (add_to_bloom, logs_bloom), and manages block/header/receipt/log/withdrawal data structures. This is the system under specification for the Osaka fork.

actors.name: Consensus Layer
actors.description: The beacon chain / consensus layer that consumes the requests_hash and deposit requests produced by the execution layer. It relies on the execution layer to faithfully extract and order deposit data. It also performs BLS signature validation for deposits. It supplies withdrawal instructions, parent_beacon_block_root, requests_hash (EIP-7685), and prev_randao to the execution layer.

actors.name: Block Builder / Proposer
actors.description: The entity responsible for constructing blocks, ordering transactions, and producing the BlockOutput (including receipts_trie and receipt_keys). Controls transaction inclusion and ordering within a block, which directly determines deposit request ordering. Sets header fields including coinbase, timestamp, extra_data, and prev_randao.

actors.name: State / Receipts Trie
actors.description: The Merkle Patricia Trie data structures (state trie for accounts, receipts trie for transaction receipts). These are trusted internal data structures that provide integrity guarantees via cryptographic roots.

actors.name: EIP-7702 Authorizer
actors.description: An account holder who signs an Authorization tuple (chain_id, address, nonce, y_parity, r, s) to delegate code execution to another address via EIP-7702 set-code transactions. This is a distinct role from the transaction sender.

actors.name: RLP Codec Library (ethereum_rlp)
actors.description: The ethereum_rlp library responsible for serializing and deserializing data structures including Receipt, Header, Block, and Account objects. Both encode_receipt and decode_receipt depend on this library for correct round-trip encoding. Bugs could cause consensus splits across clients.

actors.name: Keccak-256 Hash Function
actors.description: The cryptographic hash primitive used in bloom filter construction (add_to_bloom), account code hashing (encode_account), and transaction signing. Correctness, collision-resistance, and preimage-resistance of keccak256 are assumed.

actors.name: Smart Contract (Log Emitter)
actors.description: Deployed contract code that emits Log events (address, topics, data) during EVM execution. Logs are consumed by the bloom filter (logs_bloom) and receipt construction pipelines. The log address must match the executing contract, and topics are bounded by LOG0-LOG4 opcodes.

actors.name: P2P Network Peer
actors.description: A remote execution-layer node that transmits encoded blocks, headers, receipts, and transactions over the devp2p wire protocol. Data from peers crosses a network trust boundary and must be decoded and validated before acceptance.

trust_boundaries.description: Raw blob bytes cross into the cryptographic domain when cell_to_coset_evals (SG-001) converts byte segments into BLS field elements via bytes_to_bls_field. Malformed or adversarial blob data could encode values outside the valid field range. The assertion len(blob) == BYTES_PER_BLOB (SG-018 INV-001, SG-020 INV-002) is the first validation gate.

Deserialized field elements from cells are passed to the polynomial engine for FFT, interpolation, and evaluation. The polynomial engine trusts that input field elements are valid members of the BLS12-381 scalar field. If bytes_to_bls_field produces invalid field elements, all polynomial operations produce incorrect results.

Polynomial coefficients produced by polynomial_eval_to_coeff (SG-007) are consumed by compute_kzg_proof_multi_impl (SG-014) to generate KZG proofs. The proof generator trusts the polynomial engine to produce correct coefficient representations. An incorrect inverse FFT or bit-reversal permutation would produce invalid proofs that fail verification.

The proof generator uses KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL from the trusted setup to compute g1_lincomb (SG-014, q_commit). The entire security of the KZG commitment scheme rests on the trusted setup being honestly generated. This is a static, ceremony-level trust boundary that cannot be verified at runtime.

The batch verifier uses both KZG_SETUP_G1_MONOMIAL[:n] and KZG_SETUP_G2_MONOMIAL[n] from the trusted setup in the pairing check (SG-015). If the structured reference string is compromised, an adversary can forge proofs that pass batch verification for arbitrary data.

The batch verifier derives the random challenge r from the hash function via compute_verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_challenge (SG-006). The challenge r must be unpredictable before all inputs are committed. The Fiat-Shamir heuristic security requires the hash function to behave as a random oracle and that all verifier-relevant data is included in the hash input (SG-006 INV-001).

When externally supplied cells, commitments, and proofs are presented for batch verification (SG-021 verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch), all inputs cross from the untrusted external domain into the verifier. The verifier performs exhaustive input validation: length checks (INV-002), commitment byte length (BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT), cell index bounds (INV-004), cell byte length (BYTES_PER_CELL), and proof byte length (BYTES_PER_PROOF).

Partial cell sets provided for recovery (SG-024 recover_cells_and_kzg_proofs) cross into the recovery engine. The recovery engine validates: matching lengths (INV-001), at least 50% cell availability (INV-002), no duplicate indices (INV-003), sorted indices (INV-003), valid index bounds (INV-004), and correct cell byte lengths. A malicious requester could provide inconsistent cells that lead to recovery of an incorrect polynomial.

The recovered polynomial from recover_polynomialcoeff (SG-023) is passed to compute_cells_and_kzg_proofs_polynomialcoeff (SG-019) to regenerate all cells and proofs. If the recovery engine produces an incorrect polynomial (e.g., due to adversarial cell inputs or insufficient cell count), all regenerated proofs will be invalid.

Validators publish data column sidecars to gossip subnets (SG-006 sidecar_publishing). Each sidecar is deterministically routed to subnet compute_subnet_for_data_column_sidecar(column_index). The validator trusts the network to propagate sidecars, but the network may drop, censor, or delay them. Publishing to the wrong subnet would prevent peers from receiving the data.

The beacon state provides validator effective balances used to compute custody requirements (SG-001 get_validators_custody_requirement). Custody is proportional to stake: total_node_balance // BALANCE_PER_ADDITIONAL_CUSTODY_GROUP, clamped to [VALIDATOR_CUSTODY_REQUIREMENT, NUMBER_OF_CUSTODY_GROUPS]. The validator trusts the state to be canonical. A stale or forked state would produce incorrect custody assignments.

During the retention period (MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS), validators MUST serve sidecars on request (SG-007). Requesters are untrusted: they may flood the validator with requests (DoS), request data outside the retention window, or attempt to extract custody group membership information.

During sidecar construction (SG-002 cells_and_kzg_proofs_construction), the validator uses compute_cells to decompose blobs into cells and pairs them with proofs from blobs_bundle.proofs. The validator trusts that blobs_bundle.proofs are correctly aligned with cells: proofs[start:end] for each blob (INV-003). Misaligned proof slicing would produce sidecars with invalid cell-proof pairings.

get_data_column_sidecars_from_block (SG-004) extracts KZG commitments and computes a Merkle inclusion proof from the signed block. The block is trusted (already validated), but the Merkle proof computation is a cryptographic operation that must correctly target the blob_kzg_commitments field in the block body tree.

External user submits transaction data (including account data and EIP-7702 Authorization tuples) to the execution client. The execution client must validate all inputs before processing. This is the primary untrusted input boundary for the execution layer.

The deposit contract emits DepositEvent logs that the execution client must parse. The execution client trusts the log data format but validates all offsets and sizes rigorously via extract_deposit_data. The contract address (DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS) and event signature hash (DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH) serve as the authentication mechanism.

The execution client produces deposit_requests bytes and a requests_hash (via compute_requests_hash using SHA-256) that the consensus layer consumes. The consensus layer trusts the execution client to have faithfully extracted and ordered all deposit data from transaction receipts.

The block builder provides the BlockOutput containing receipt_keys ordering and the receipts_trie. The execution client trusts receipt_keys ordering to determine deposit request ordering, and trusts that trie_get returns valid receipt data for each key.

An EIP-7702 authorizer signs an Authorization tuple with ECDSA (y_parity, r, s) delegating code to a target address. The execution client must verify the signature, chain_id for replay protection, and nonce for freshness before accepting the authorization.

The execution client reads from and writes to the state and receipts tries. Account encoding (encode_account) produces RLP data stored in the state trie. The storage_root parameter is a 32-byte Merkle root that must be externally supplied and correct.

External users interact with the deposit contract to make beacon chain deposits. The contract emits logs that later get parsed by the execution client. The user trusts the contract to correctly emit deposit events with proper BLS public keys, withdrawal credentials, amounts, signatures, and indices.

Transaction submission boundary for blocks.py. Raw transactions from EOAs enter the execution engine for validation and inclusion. The transaction carries a type discriminator (Legacy/AccessList/FeeMarket/Blob/SetCode) and a cryptographic signature (v/r/s or y_parity). The execution engine must validate the transaction type, decode it correctly, and recover the sender via ECDSA before trusting any field.

Block construction boundary. The proposer supplies a Block containing a Header (21 fields), a list of transactions (typed or legacy), ommers (always empty post-merge), and withdrawals. The execution engine must validate every header field against consensus rules, verify transaction ordering, and ensure the proposer has not manipulated coinbase, timestamp, or extra_data.

Consensus-to-execution layer boundary for withdrawals and beacon data. The beacon chain supplies Withdrawal objects (index, validator_index, address, amount), parent_beacon_block_root, and requests_hash (EIP-7685). The execution layer trusts these values as authoritative but must validate their format and consistency with header commitments.

Log emission boundary. Smart contracts emit Log events (address, topics, data) during EVM execution. These logs are consumed by logs_bloom for bloom filter construction and by receipt_construction for inclusion in Receipt objects.
